question: Two inductors $$L_{1}$$ and $$L_{2}$$ are connected in parallel and a time varying current flows as shown. The ratio $$\dfrac{i_{1}}{i_{2}}$$ is equal to
explanation:
The inductors are in parallel. Therefore, potential difference across them is same.
$$L_{1}\left(\dfrac{d i_{1}}{d t}\right)=L_{2}\left(\dfrac{d i_{2}}{d t}\right)$$
or $$\quad L_{1}\left(d i_{1}\right)=L_{2}\left(d i_{2}\right)$$
Integrating, we get
$$L_{1} i_{1}=L_{2} i_{2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{i_{1}}{i_{2}}=\dfrac{L_{2}}{L_{1}}$$
subject: Physics

question: Two identical induction coils each of inductance $L$ joined in series are placed very close to each other such that the winding direction of one is exactly opposite to that of the other. What is the net inductance?
explanation:
When the two coils are joined in series such that the winding of one is opposite to the other, then the emf produced in first coil is $$180^{\circ}$$ out of phase of the emf produced in second coil.
$$\begin{aligned}L_{\text {net }} & =L_{1}+L_{2} \\& =-\dfrac{\phi}{i}+\dfrac{\phi}{i}=0\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: The equivalent inductance of two inductances is 2.4 henry when connected in parallel and 10 henry when connected in series. The difference between the two inductances is
subject: Physics

explanation:

$$\Rightarrow L_{e q}=\dfrac{L}{3}=\dfrac{3}{3}=1 H$$

question: A coil of resistance $50 \Omega$ is connected across a  $5.0{\text{ }}V$  battery. If the current in the coil is found to be  $50{\text{ }}mA$  after time $t=0.1 {~s}$ battery is connected, then the inductance of the coil is
explanation:
Given, resistance, $R=50 \Omega$
supply voltage, $V=5 {~V}$
current, $i=50 {~mA}=50 \times 10^{-3} {~A}$
Time taken, $t=0.1 s$
As we know that,
$i = {i_0}\left( {1 - {e^{ - \frac{t}{\tau }}}} \right)\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
where, $i_{0}=$ peak current,
$i=$ current in coil
and $\tau=$ time constant $=\dfrac{L}{R}$.
Since, by using Ohm's law,
$V=i_{0} R$
$\Rightarrow i_{0}=\dfrac{V}{R}=\dfrac{5}{50}$
Put in Eq. (1), we get
$\Rightarrow 50 \times 10^{-3}=\dfrac{5}{50}\left(1-e^{-\dfrac{0.1}{\tau}}\right)$
$\Rightarrow 5 \times 10^{-3+2}=1-e^{-0.1 / \tau}$
$\Rightarrow \quad e^{-0.1 / \tau}=1-0.5=0.5$
Taking log both sides, we get
$\Rightarrow-\dfrac{0 \cdot 1}{\tau} \log _{e} e=\ln \dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow-\dfrac{0 \cdot 1}{\tau}=\ln 1-\ln 2$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{0.1}{\tau}=\ln 2 \Rightarrow \tau=\dfrac{0.1}{\ln 2}=\dfrac{L}{R}$
$\Rightarrow \quad L=\dfrac{0.1 R}{\ln 2}=\dfrac{0.1 \times 50}{\ln 2}=\dfrac{5}{\ln 2}$
Hence, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: In given  DC  circuit, find current for $R=12 \Omega$ in steady state.
explanation: In steady state condition an inductor acts like a conducting wire. So the net current through the circuit is $i = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{{12}}{{12}} = 1\,A$
subject: Physics

question: An inductance of negligible resistance whose reactance is ${120\,\, \Omega}$ at 200 $Hz$ is connected to a ${240 {~V}, 60 {~Hz}}$ power line. What is the current in the inductor  ?
explanation:
The reactance $\left(X_{{L}}\right)$ of the inductance at 200 $Hz$ is $120 \Omega$.
$\begin{aligned}\text { As } X_{L} & =\omega L=2 \pi \nu \times L \\L & =\dfrac{X_{L}}{2 \pi v}=\dfrac{120}{2 \pi \times 200}=\dfrac{3}{10 \pi}\end{aligned}$
If $X_{L}^{\prime}$ denotes the reactance of the same inductance at 60 $Hz$ ,
$\begin{aligned}X_{L}^{\prime} & =\omega^{\prime} L=2 \pi v^{\prime} \times L \\\text { or } \quad X_{L}^{\prime} & =2 \pi \times 60 \times \dfrac{3}{10 \pi}=36 \Omega\end{aligned}$
If $I_{\text {rms }}$ is current that flows through the inductance when connected to 240 $V$ and 60 $Hz$ power line, then$I_{{rms}}=\dfrac{\varepsilon_{{rms}}}{X_{L}{ }^{\prime}}=\dfrac{240 {~V}}{36 \Omega}=6.66 {~A}$
subject: Physics

question:
Figure shows a circuit having a coil of resistance ${R=2.5 \Omega}$ and inductance ${L}$ connected to a conducting rod ${P Q}$ which can slide on a perfectly conducting circular ring of radius 10 $cm$ with its center at ${P}$. Assume that friction and gravity are absent and a constant uniform magnetic field of 5 $T$ exists as shown in figure.

At ${t=0}$, the circuit is switched on and simultaneously a time-varying external torque is applied on the rod so that it rotates about ${P}$ with a constant angular velocity $40\,rad{s^{ - 1}}.$ Find the magnitude of this torque when current reaches half of its maximum value. Neglect the self inductance of the loop formed by the circuit.
explanation:
Induced $E M F=\dfrac{1}{2} B \omega l^{2}$
Maximum current: $i_{0}=\dfrac{B \omega l^{2}}{2 R}$
Torque about the hinge $P$ is
$\tau=\int_{0}^{1} i(d x) B x \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau=\dfrac{1}{2} i B l^{2}$
Putting $i=i_{0} / 2$, we get: $\tau=\dfrac{B^{2} \omega l^{4}}{8 R}=5 {mNm}$
subject: Physics

question: In the circuit shown in figure, find the magnitude of the potential of point ${C}$ at the instant shown.   The rate of increase of current in resistance is ${2.5 {~A} / {s}}$.
explanation:
The potential of point $D$ is equal to zero. Thus, for the 4 $H$ inductor we can write
$L \dfrac{d i_{2}}{d t}=V_{D}-V_{B}=-40 \Rightarrow \dfrac{d i_{2}}{d t}=-10 {~A} / {s}$
At the junction $D$ by $KCL$ we have. $i_{1}=i_{2}=i_{3}$$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{d i_{1}}{d t}=\dfrac{d i_{2}}{d t}+\dfrac{d i_{3}}{d t} \\& \Rightarrow \quad 2.5=-10+\dfrac{d i_{3}}{d t} \Rightarrow \dfrac{d i_{3}}{d t}=12.5 {~A} / {s}\end{aligned}$
Writing equation of potential drop from point $D$ to $C$ gives $V_{D}+10-2 \times 12.5=V_{C} \Rightarrow V_{C}=-15 {~V}$
subject: Physics

question: In given figure, $R = 15\,\Omega ,L = 5.0\,H$, the ideal battery has ${\varepsilon=10 {~V}}$, and the fuse in the upper branch is an ideal $3.0\,A$ fuse. It has zero resistance as long as the current through it remains less than $3.0\,A.$ If the current reaches $3.0\,A,$ the fuse "blows" and thereafter has infinite resistance. Switch ${S}$ is closed at time ${t=0}$. At what time does the fuse blow ?
explanation:
Before the fuse blows, the current through the resistor remains zero. We apply the loop theorem to the battery-fuse-inductor loop: $\varepsilon - L\,di/dt = 0$. So ${i=\varepsilon t / L}$. As the fuse blows at ${t=t_{0}}$, ${i=i_{0}=3.0 {~A}}$. Thus,
$t_{0}=\dfrac{i_{0} L}{\varepsilon}=\dfrac{(3.0 {~A})(5.0 {H})}{10 {~V}}=1.5 {~s}$
subject: Physics

question: When the current in the portion of the circuit shown in figure is $2.00\,A$ and increases at a rate of ${0.500 {~A} / {s}}$, the measured voltage is ${\Delta V_{a b}=9.00 {~V}}$. When the current is $2.00\,A$ and decreases at the rate of ${0.500 {~A} / {s}}$, the measured voltage is ${\Delta V_{a b}=5.00 {~V}}$. Calculate the value  of inductance
explanation:
The voltages are related as
$\varepsilon-i R-L \dfrac{d i}{d t}=0 \Rightarrow \varepsilon=i R+L \dfrac{d i}{d t}=0$
When the current is increasing
$9.0 = (2.0)R + L(0.50){\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
When the current is decreasing:
$5.0 = (2.0)R + L( - 0.50){\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Subtracting (2) from (1) gives
$9.0-5.0-L(1.0) \Rightarrow L=4.0 {H}$
subject: Physics

question: In the following circuit, the switch is closed at ${t}=0$. Initially, there is no current in inductor. The relation of current in the coil with time is given as ${i}=\dfrac{\varepsilon}{{NR}}\left[1-\exp \left(-{Q} \dfrac{{R}}{{L}} {t}\right)\right]$. What is the value of $\dfrac{{N}}{{Q}}$ ?
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956508/preptracker/questions/q_43963_explanation_1764956507758.png
At any time $t$, applying Kirchhoff's law in loop $AFEBA$,
$-\varepsilon+i_{1} R-\left(i-i_{1}\right) R=0$
$\Rightarrow-\varepsilon+2 i_{1} R-i R=0$
$\Rightarrow i_{1}=\dfrac{i R+\varepsilon}{2 R}$
Now, applying Kirchhoff's law in loop $AFEDCBA$,
$\Rightarrow-\varepsilon+{i}_{1} {R}+{iR}+{L} \dfrac{{di}}{{dt}}=0$
$\Rightarrow-\varepsilon+\left(\dfrac{{iR}+\varepsilon}{2 {R}}\right) {R}+{iR}+{L} \dfrac{{di}}{{dt}}=0$
$\Rightarrow-\varepsilon+\left(\dfrac{{iR}+\varepsilon}{2}\right)+{iR}+{L} \dfrac{{di}}{{dt}}=0$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{-\varepsilon}{2}+\dfrac{3 {iR}}{2}=-{L} \dfrac{{di}}{{dt}}$
$ \Rightarrow \left( {\frac{{ - \varepsilon + 3iR}}{2}} \right)dt = - Ldi$
Integrating both sides, under proper limit,
$\Rightarrow-\int_{0}^{1} \dfrac{{dt}}{2 {~L}}=\int_{0}^{{i}} \dfrac{{di}}{-\varepsilon+3 {iR}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{-{t}}{2 {~L}}=\dfrac{1}{3 {R}} \ln \left(\dfrac{-\varepsilon+3 {iR}}{-\varepsilon}\right)$
$\Rightarrow-\ln \left(\dfrac{-\varepsilon+3 {iR}}{-\varepsilon}\right)=\dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{{Rt}}{{L}}$
Taking antilogarithms on both sides,
$ \Rightarrow 1 - \frac{{3iR}}{\varepsilon } = \exp \left( {\frac{{ - 3}}{2}\frac{{Rt}}{L}} \right)$
$\Rightarrow {i}=\dfrac{\varepsilon}{3 {R}}\left[1-\exp \left(\dfrac{-3}{2} \dfrac{{Rt}}{{L}}\right)\right]$
$\therefore \quad {N}=3 ; {Q}=\dfrac{3}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{{N}}{{Q}}=\dfrac{3}{\dfrac{3}{2}}=\dfrac{6}{3}=2$
subject: Physics

question: An inductor coil stores $32 {~J}$ of magnetic field energy and dissipates energy as heat at the rate of $320 {~W}$ when a current of $4 {~A}$ is passed through it. The time constant of the circuit when this coil is joined across an ideal battery is
explanation:
The magnetic field energy stored in an inductor is $U = \frac{1}{2}L{I^2}$
The power dissipated as heat is given by
${P}={I}^{2} {R}$
Time constant of the circuit is$\tau=\dfrac{{L}}{{R}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{2 {U}}{{I}^{2}}}{\dfrac{{P}}{{I}^{2}}}=\dfrac{2 {U}}{{P}}=\dfrac{2 \times 32}{320}=0.2 {~s}$
subject: Physics

question:
A 30 Henry inductor coil is connected to a $20 \,\Omega$ resistance in series (as shown in figure). The time at which rate of dissipation of energy (Joule's heat) across resistance is equal to the rate at which magnetic energy is stored in the inductor, is
[Take $l$$n2 = 0.69$]
explanation:
$L = 30\,H,R = 20\,\Omega $
According to question
$\begin{aligned} & \text { rate of Joule's } \\ & \text { heat dissipation }\end{aligned}=\begin{aligned} & \text { rate of magnetic } \\ & \text { energy stored }\end{aligned}$
$ \Rightarrow {I^2}R = \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {\frac{1}{2}L{I^2}} \right)$
$\Rightarrow {I}^{2} {R}=\dfrac{1}{2} {~L}(2 {I}) \dfrac{{dI}}{{dt}}$
$ \Rightarrow R{I_0}\left[ {1 - \exp \left( {\frac{{ - t}}{\tau }} \right)} \right]$
$ = L{I_0}\left[ { - \exp \left( {\frac{{ - t}}{\tau }} \right)} \right]\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{\tau }} \right)$
$\left. {\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\because \,\,\,\,\,I = {I_0}\left[ {1 - \exp \left( {\frac{{ - t}}{\tau }} \right)} \right]} \right]$
$\Rightarrow \exp \left(\dfrac{{t}}{\tau}\right)=\dfrac{{L}}{\tau {R}}+1=1+1$$\left. {\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\because \tau = \frac{L}{R}} \right]$
$\Rightarrow {t}=\tau \ln 2$
$=\dfrac{{L}}{{R}} \ln 2=\dfrac{30}{20} \times 0.69$
$=1.04$ units.
subject: Physics

question: In the branch ${A B}$ of a circuit, as shown in the figure, a current ${I=(t+2) A}$ is flowing where ${t}$ is the time in second. At ${t=0}$, the value of ${\left(V_{A}-V_{B}\right)}$ will be
explanation:
On applying ${K V L}$ rule,
${V_{A}-I R-L \dfrac{d I}{d t}+10=V_{B} \Rightarrow V_{A}-V_{B}=-3 V}$
subject: Physics

question: An ideal coil of $10\,H$ is connected in series with a resistance of $5\,\Omega $ and a battery of $5\,V$. After $2\,s$, after the connection is made, the current flowing (in ampere) in the circuit is
explanation:
Rise of current in $L-R$ circuit is given by
$I=I_{0}\left(1-e^{-t / \tau}\right)$
where, ${I_0} = \frac{E}{R} = \frac{5}{5} = 1\,A$
Now, $\tau = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{{10}}{5} = 2\,s$
After $2\,s$, i.e. at $t = 2\,s$
Rise of current, $I=\left(1-e^{-1}\right) A$
subject: Physics

question: In the circuit shown, what is the energy stored in the coil at steady state?
explanation: Four resistance form a balanced wheat stone bridge. So no current will flow through the inductor.

question: In the circuits (1) and (2) switches $$S_{1}$$ and $$S_{2}$$ are closed at $$t=0$$ and are kept closed for a long time. The variation of currents in the two circuits for $$t \geq 0$$ are roughly shown by (figures are schematic and not drawn to scale) : (a)
explanation:
Circuit $$-a$$
The current is $$i=i_{0} e^{-t / R C}$$
$$\text { At } t=0, i=i_{o}$$
$$\text { As } t \rightarrow \infty i=0$$
Circuit -b
The current is $$i=i_{0}\left(1-e^{-\dfrac{R t}{L}}\right)$$
At $$t=0 \Rightarrow i=0$$
As $$t \rightarrow \infty \Rightarrow i=i_{0}$$
so the currect graph is (2)
subject: Physics

question: In the circuit shown below, the key $K$ is closed at $$t=0$$. The current through the battery is
explanation:
At $$t=0$$, the inductor behaves like an infinite
resistance. So at $$\mathrm{t}=0, i=\mathrm{V} / \mathrm{R}_{2}$$ and at $$t=\infty$$, the inductor behaves like a conducting wire. So
$$i=\dfrac{V}{R_{e q}}=\dfrac{V\left(R_{1}+R_{2}\right)}{R_{1} R_{2}}$$
subject: Physics

question: An inductor $$(L=0.03 H)$$ and a resistor $(R = 0.15\,k\Omega )$ are connected in series to a battery of $15\,\,V$ e.m.f in a circuit shown. The key $$k_{1}$$ has been kept closed for a long time. Then at $t = 0,\,{K_1}$ is opened and key $$K_{2}$$ is closed simultaneously. At $t = 1\,\,ms$, the current in the circuit will be: $$\left(e^{5} \cong 150\right)$$
explanation:
When $$K_{1}$$ is closed and $$K_{2}$$ is open, $$I_{0}=\dfrac{E}{R}$$
when $$K_{1}$$ is open and $$K_{2}$$ is closed, current as a function of time ' $t$ ' in $$L R$$ circuit is
$I = {I_0}{e^{ - R\frac{t}{L}}} = \frac{1}{{10}}{e^{ - 5}} = \frac{1}{{1500}} = 0.67\,mA$
subject: Physics

question: An inductor $L$, a resistance $R$ and two identical bulbs, $$B_{1}$$ and $$B_{2}$$ are connected to a battery through a switch $S$ as shown in the figure. Which of the following statements gives the correct description of the happenings when the switch $S$ is closed.
explanation: After the switch $$\delta$$ is closed at $t = 0$ the inductor does not allow the current through it and hence bulb $$B_{2}$$ glows first. After long time the inductor behaves like a conductor and hence resistance of the upper wire is less and current through $$B_{1}$$ is more.

options.text: $$B_{1}$$ and $$B_{2}$$ light up together with equal brightness all the time.
The bulb $$B_{2}$$ lights up earlier than $$B_{1}$$ and finally both the bulbs shine equally bright.
$$B_{1}$$ light up earlier and finally both the bulbs acquire equal brightness.
$$B_{2}$$ lights up earlier and finally $$B_{1}$$ shines brighter than $$B_{2}$$.

subject: Physics

question: An inductor of inductance $L = 400\,mH$ and resistors of resistance $$R_{1}=2 \Omega$$ and $$R_{2}=2 \Omega$$ are connected to a battery of emf $12\,\,V$ as shown in the figure. The internal resistance of the battery in negligible. The switch $$S$$ is closed at $$t=0$$. The potential drop across $$L$$ as a function of time is
explanation:
The current through the inductor as a function of time is
$i = {i_{\max }}\left[ {1 - {e^{ - \frac{{R't}}{L}}}} \right]$
Where $$i_{\max }$$ is the maximum current through the inductor in steady state.
$$R^{\prime}$$ is the equivalent resistance of the circuit across the inductor.
At steady state
$$i_{\max }=\dfrac{E}{R_{2}}$$
The equivalent resistance $$R^{\prime}$$ is $$R^{\prime}=R_{2}$$
The current through the inductor is
$i = \frac{E}{{{R_2}}}\left[ {1 - {e^{ - \frac{{Rt}}{L}}}} \right]$
The potential difference across the inductor is
${V_L} = L\frac{{di}}{{dt}} = E{e^{ - \frac{{{R_2}t}}{L}}} = 12{e^{ - 5t}}\,V$
subject: Physics

question: In the circuit shown here, the point ' $C$ ' is kept connected to point ' $$A$$ ' till the current flowing through the circuit becomes constant. Afterward, suddenly, point ' $C$ ' is disconnected from point ' $A$ ' and connected to point ' $B$ ' at time $t = 0$. Ratio of the voltage across resistance and the inductor at $t = L/R$ will be equal to:
explanation:
The voltages across resistor and inductor are
${V_R} = iR = \left( {\frac{\varepsilon }{R}{e^{ - \frac{{Rt}}{L}}}} \right)R = \varepsilon {e^{ - \frac{R}{L}t}}$
${V_L} = L\frac{{di}}{{dt}} = L\frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {\frac{\varepsilon }{R}{e^{ - \frac{R}{L}t}}} \right) = \varepsilon {e^{ - \frac{R}{L}t}}$
$\frac{{{V_R}}}{{{V_L}}} = 1$
subject: Physics

question: An emf of $15\,\,V$ is applied in a circuit containing $5\,H$ inductance and $$10 \Omega$$, the ratio of currents at time $$t=\infty$$ and $t = 1\,\,s$ is
explanation:
The curent through the circuit is
$$i=i_{o}\left(1-e^{\frac{-R}{L} t}\right)=i_{o}\left(1-e^{-2 t}\right)$$
At $$t=1 s i_{1}=i_{o}\left(1-\dfrac{1}{e^{2}}\right)$$
As $$t \rightarrow \infty$$
$$\dfrac{i_{2}}{i_{1}}=\dfrac{e^{2}}{e^{2}-1} \Rightarrow \phi=L_{1} i_{1}=L_{2} i_{2}$$
subject: Physics

question: A coil of inductance $0.20\,H$ is connected in series with a switch and a cell of emf $1.6\,\,V$. The total resistance of the circuit is $$4 \Omega$$. What is the initial rate of growth of the current when the switch is closed?

explanation: Steady state current does not depend on inductance of the inductor. Current $$i$$ in the circuit is $$i=\dfrac{E}{R}$$

question:
In an $L R$ circuit, the resistance $R=0.2 \Omega$. At a certain instant $\left( {{V_A} - {V_B}} \right) = 0.5\,V,I = 0.5\,A{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{and}}{\mkern 1mu} $
${\mkern 1mu} (\Delta I/\Delta t) = 8A/s$. Find the inductance of the coil:
explanation:
$\left|V_{A}-V_{B}\right|=V_{L}+V_{R}=L \dfrac{d I}{d t}+I R$
or $\quad 0.5 \mathrm{~V}=L \times 8+0.5 \times 0.2$
Solving, we get $L = 0.05\,H$
subject: Physics

question: An inductor of 2 henry and a resistance of 10 ohms are connected in series with a battery of 5 volts. The initial rate of change of current is:
explanation:
$$i=i_{0}\left(1-e^{\frac{-R t}{L}}\right)$$
$$\dfrac{d i}{d t}=-i_{0}\left(-\dfrac{R}{L}\right) e^{\frac{-R t}{L}}=\dfrac{i_{0} R}{L} \cdot e^{\frac{-R t}{L}}$$
At $t = 0$
$\frac{{di}}{{dt}} = \frac{{{i_0}R}}{L} = \frac{E}{L} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5\,amp/\sec $
subject: Physics

question: The network shown in the figure is a part of a complete circuit. If at a certain instant the current $i$ is $5\,A$ and is decreasing at the rate of ${10^3}\,A/S\,{\text{then}}\,{V_A} - {V_B}$ is:
explanation:
By using Kirchoff's voltage law
$$\begin{aligned}& V_{A}-i R+E-L \dfrac{d i}{d t}=V_{B} \\& \dfrac{d i}{d t}=-10^{3} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$$
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_08_16_48f85480386a4e05bde8g-4.jpg?height=122&width=413&top_left_y=1850&top_left_x=1324
$ \Rightarrow {V_B} - {V_A} = 15\,volt.$
subject: Physics

question: Figure shows a circuit that contains three identical resistors with resistance $R = 9.0\Omega $ each, two identical inductors with inductance $L = 2.0\,mH$ each, and an ideal battery with emf $\varepsilon  = 18\,V$. The current ' $i$ ' through the battery just after the switch closed is,
explanation:
${\text{At}}\,t = 0$ inductor behaves like an infinite resistor and hence it can be disconnected. A capacitor behaves like a conductor. The circuit can be redrawn as
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957115/preptracker/questions/q_43008_explanation_1764957115454.png
${i_1} = \frac{\varepsilon }{R} = \frac{{18}}{9} = 2\,A$

Alter
According to question language:
Capacitor is not mentioned so $i = 2\,\,A$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The growth of current in R-L circuit is uniform.


Reason: Inductor $$(L)$$ opposes the growth of current.
explanation:
The current in R-L circuit grows exponentially
$$I=I_{0}\left(1-e^{-R t / L}\right) .$$
So option (4) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The quantity $L/R$ possesses dimension of time.

Reason: To reduce the rate of growth of current through a solenoid, we should increase the time constant $(L/R).$
explanation:
The current through an inductor is
$$I=I_{0}\left[1-e^{\frac{-t}{\tau}}\right]$$
$$\dfrac{d I}{d t}=\dfrac{I_{0}}{\tau} e^{-\dfrac{t}{\tau}} \text { where } \tau=\dfrac{L}{R}$$
when $$\tau$$ increases $$\dfrac{d I}{d t}$$ decreases.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: An ideal coil of $10\,H$ is connected in series with a resistance of $$5 \Omega$$ and a battery of $5\,V$. Two seconds after the connection is made, the current flowing (in $A$) in the circuit is
explanation: $$I=\dfrac{E}{R}\left(1-e^{R t / L}\right)=\dfrac{5 V}{5 \Omega}\left[1-e^{\frac{-(5) 2}{10}}\right]=\left[1-e^{-1}\right]$$
subject: Physics

question: In the circuit shown, the switch $$S$$ is shifted to position 2 from position 1 at $$t=0$$, having been in position 1 for a long time. Find the current in the circuit as a function of time.
explanation:
Let $$I_{0}$$ be the initial current in the steady state condition before shifting the switch.
$$\therefore \quad I_{0}=\dfrac{\varepsilon}{R}$$
Let $I$ be the current in the circuit at time $t = 0$ Since the flux associated with inductors will be same just before and after shifting of switch, (otherwise the induced emf would be infinite).
$$\begin{aligned}& \phi=I_{0} L=I(L+2 L) \\& \Rightarrow I=\dfrac{I_{0}}{3}=\dfrac{\varepsilon}{3 R}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: In the circuit shown, sliding contact is moving with uniform velocity towards right. The value of resistance at some instance is $$12 \Omega$$. The current in the circuit at this instant of time will be
explanation:
Since resistance of the circuit is increasing and hence current in the circuit is decreasing so $$\dfrac{d i}{d t}$$ is negative.
Current in the circuit is given by $$i=\dfrac{6-L \dfrac{d i}{d t}}{12}$$ Since $$\dfrac{d i}{d t}$$ is negative so the value of numerator will be more than $6\,\,V$ and hence current in the circuit at that instant will be more than $0.5\,A.$

options.text: May be less or more than $0.5\,A$ depending on the value of  $L.$

subject: Physics

question: In the circuit given below, switch $$S$$ is closed for sufficiently long time. At $$t=0$$, the switch is opened, then
explanation:
Current through the inductor at $t = 0,$
$$i_{0}=\dfrac{E}{R_{1}}$$
$$\therefore$$ Current at time $t$
$$\begin{aligned}& i=i_{0} e^{-t / \tau} \\& i=i_{o} e^{-1}=\dfrac{E}{e R_{1}} \quad(\text { At } t=\tau)\end{aligned}$$
Also $$\quad \tau=\dfrac{L}{R_{1}+R_{2}}$$

options.text:
The current after one time constant is
$$E / e\left(R_{1}+R_{2}\right)$$

The current after one time constant is

question: The current in a $$L R$$ circuit builds up to $\frac{3}{4}th$ of its steady state value in $4\,s$. The time constant of this circuit is
explanation:
We know that $$i=i_{0}\left[1-e^{\frac{-R t}{L}}\right]$$
$$\text { or } \dfrac{3}{4} i_{0}=i_{0}\left[1-e^{-t / \tau}\right]$$
(Where $$\tau=\dfrac{L}{R}=$$ time constant )
$$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{3}{4}=1-e^{-t / \tau} \text { or }^{-t / \tau}=\dfrac{1}{4} \\& e^{t / \tau}=4 \text { or } \dfrac{t}{\tau}=\operatorname{In} 4 \\& \Rightarrow \tau=\dfrac{t}{\operatorname{In} 4}=\dfrac{4}{2 \operatorname{In} 2} \Rightarrow \tau=\dfrac{2}{\operatorname{In} 2} \mathrm{sec} .\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: A coil of inductance $8.4mH$ and resistance $$6 \Omega$$ is connected to a $$12 \mathrm{~V}$$ battery. The current in the coil is $1.0\,A$ in the time (apporox.)
explanation:

$$\begin{aligned}& i=\dfrac{\varepsilon}{R}\left[1-e^{\frac{-R}{L} t}\right]=\dfrac{12}{6}\left[1-e^{\frac{-R}{L} t}\right] \\& 1=2\left[1-e^{\frac{-R}{L} t}\right] \\& e^{\frac{-R}{L} t}=\dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{\ln (2) L}{R}=1 m s\end{aligned}$$

question: The time constant of an inductor is $$\tau_{1}$$. When a pure resistor of $R\Omega $ is connected in series with it, the time constant is found to decrease to $$\tau_{2}$$. The internal resistance of the inductor is
explanation:

From the above two equations
$$r=\dfrac{\tau_{2} R}{\tau_{1}-\tau_{2}}$$

question: In the circuit shown in figure switch $S$ is closed at time $$t=0$$. The charge which passes through the battery in one time constant is
explanation:
In L-R circuit, the growing current at time $t$ is given by
$$i=i_{0}\left[1-e^{-t / \tau}\right] \text { where, } i_{0}=\dfrac{E}{R} \text { and } \tau=\dfrac{L}{R}$$
$$\therefore$$ Charge passed through the battery in one time constant is
$$\begin{aligned}& q=\int_{0}^{\tau} i d t=\int_{0}^{\tau} i_{0}\left(1-e^{-t / \tau}\right) d t \\& q=i_{0} \tau+i_{0} \tau\left[e^{-1}-1\right]=\dfrac{i_{0} \tau}{e} \\& q=\dfrac{i_{0} \tau}{e}=\dfrac{(E / R)(L / R)}{e}=\dfrac{E L}{e R^{2}}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

Assertion: A metallic surface is moved in and out in magnetic field then emf is induced in it.

Reason: Eddy current will be produced in a metallic surface moving in and out of magnetic field.
explanation:
When a metallic surface is moved in and out of a magnetic field, the magnetic flux linked with the metallic surface changes continuously. According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, a change in magnetic flux induces an electromotive force (emf) in the closed loop (the metallic surface, in this case). As a result, an induced current, known as eddy current, starts flowing in the metallic surface.
Because it provides a valid explanation for why an emf is induced in the metallic surface when it is moved in and out of a magnetic field. Therefore, both the Assertion and Reason are correct, and the Reason is the correct explanation for the Assertion.

B matches with P because the armature, or the coil, in an AC generator is rotated within a magnetic field to convert mechanical energy into electrical energy.

D matches with R because Nicola Tesla is credited with the development of the AC generator, which fundamentally changed the generation and distribution of alternating current electricity. Correct option is (2).

question: A coil having number of turns $N$ and cross-sectional area $A$ is rotated in a uniform magnetic field $B$ with an angular velocity $\omega$. The maximum value of the emf induced in it is -
explanation:
The flux linking with the coil at any instant $t$ is given as
$\Phi = NBA\cos \omega t$
$\therefore \varepsilon = - \frac{{d\Phi }}{{dt}} = NBA\sin \omega t$
Therefore, the maximum value of $emf$ is ${\varepsilon _{\max }} = NBA\,\omega $

question:
Assertion: An electric motor will have maximum efficiency when back emf becomes equal to half of applied $emf.$

Reason: Efficiency of electric motor does not depend on magnitude of back $emf.$
explanation: Maximum efficiency in an electric motor is achieved when the back electromotive force (back emf) approaches the applied electromotive force (applied emf). Specifying an exact ratio of "half" is an approximation and might not hold true for all motor designs and applications. The reason is inaccurate in stating that motor efficiency depends solely on the magnitude of back emf. In reality, motor efficiency is influenced by various other factors, such as resistive losses, mechanical friction losses. Assertion is true but reason is false. So, correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: An electric motor will have maximum efficiency when back emf becomes equal to half of applied $emf$.

Reason: Efficiency of electric motor depends only on magnitude of back $emf$.
explanation:
An electric motor will have maximum efficiency when the back electromotive force (emf) becomes equal to half of the applied emf. This is because at this point, the motor is operating at an optimal balance between back emf and applied emf, maximizing the efficiency of the motor.
The efficiency of an electric motor depends on several factors, not just the magnitude of back emf. It also depends on factors such as the resistance in the motor windings and the mechanical load on the motor. The relationship between back emf and applied emf is just one aspect of motor efficiency, but it does not solely determine motor efficiency. Therefore, while the assertion is correct, the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.

question: Consider two solenoids $X$ and $Y$ such that the area and length of $Y$ are twice as that of $X$ respectively and the magnetic energy    stored in both the solenoids is same, then the ratio of magnitude of magnetic fields of the two solenoids, $\dfrac{\left|B_{X}\right|}{\left|B_{Y}\right|}$ is
explanation:
According to given condition,
Magnetic energy stored in solenoid $X=$
Magnetic energy stored in solenoid $Y$
$\Rightarrow U_{X}=U_{Y}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{B_X^2{V_X}}}{{2{\mu _0}}} = \frac{{B_Y^2{V_Y}}}{{2{\mu _0}}}\quad \left[ {\because \,\,U = \frac{{{B^2}V}}{{2{\mu _0}}},V = {\text{volume}}} \right]$
$B_{X}^{2} V_{X}=B_{Y}^{2} V_{Y}$
$\Rightarrow B_{X}^{2} A_{X} L_{X}=B_{Y}^{2} A_{Y} L_{Y}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{B_X^2}}{{B_Y^2}} = \frac{{{A_Y}}}{{{A_X}}} \times \frac{{{L_Y}}}{{{L_X}}} = \frac{{2{A_X}}}{{{A_X}}} \times \frac{{2{L_X}}}{{{L_X}}}$ $ \Rightarrow \frac{{B_X^2}}{{B_\gamma ^2}} = 4$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{B_{X}}{B_{Y}}=\sqrt{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{B_{X}}{B_{Y}}=2$
$\Rightarrow B_{X}: B_{Y}=2: 1$
Hence, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A long coaxial cable consists of two thin-walled conducting cylinders with inner radius 2 $cm$ and outer radius 8 $cm$ . The inner cylinder carries a steady current 0.1 $A$ , and the outer cylinder provides the return path for that current. The current produces a magnetic field between the two cylinders. Find the energy stored in the magnetic field for length 5 $m$ of the cable. Express answer  (use ${\ln 2=0.7}$ )
explanation:
The magnetic field inside is only dueto the current of the inner cylinder.https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956525/preptracker/questions/q_44100_explanation_1764956524718.png $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \pi r}$
Magnetic field energy density is not uniform in the space between the cylinders. At a distance $r$ from the centre,
$u_{B}=\dfrac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i^{2}}{8 \pi^{2} r^{2}}$
Energy in volume of element (length $l\,)$
$d{U_B} = {u_B}dV = \frac{{{\mu _0}{i^2}}}{{8{\pi ^2}{r^2}}}(2\pi rl)dr = \frac{{{\mu _0}{i^2}l}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{dr}}{r}$
${U_B} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{i^2}l}}{{4\pi }}\int_a^b {\frac{{dr}}{r}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{i^2}l}}{{4\pi }}\ln \frac{b}{a}$
Using values, we get $U=7 {~nJ}$
subject: Physics

question: An electromagnet has stored $648\,\,J$ of magnetic energy when a current of 9 $A$ exists in its coils. What average emf is induced if the current is reduced to zero in 0.45 ?
explanation:

$$\therefore L=\dfrac{2 U}{I^{2}}=\dfrac{2 \times 648}{(9)^{2}}=16 H$$
Induced emf,
$\varepsilon = - L\frac{{dI}}{{dt}} = \frac{{ - (16H)(0 - 9A)}}{{0.45s}} = 320\,V$

question: A long solenoid with 40 turns per $cm$ carries a current of  $1\,A$. The magnetic energy stored per unit volume is $J/{m^3}.$

$ = 2\pi \times {10^{ - 7}} \times {\left( {4 \times {{10}^3}} \right)^2} \times {1^2}$
$ = 2\pi \times 16 \times {10^{ - 7 + 6}} = 3.2\pi \,J{m^{ - 3}}$

question: A physicist works in a laboratory where the magnetic field is $2\,T$. She wears a necklace enclosing area $0.01\,{m^2}$ in such a way that the plane of the necklace is normal to the field and is having a resistance $$R=0.01 \Omega$$. Because of power failure, the field decays to $1\,T$ in time $$10^{-3}$$ seconds. Then what is the total heat produced in her necklace?
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned}& H=\dfrac{V^{2} t}{R} \text { and } V=\dfrac{N\left(B_{2}-B_{1}\right) A}{t} \\& V=\dfrac{1 \times(1-2) \times 0.01}{10^{-3}}=10 V\end{aligned}$$
and, $\quad H = \frac{{{{(10)}^2} \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{0.01}} = 10\,\,J$

question: A wheel with $N$ spokes each of length $L m$ is rotated with a uniform angular velocity $\omega$ in a plane normal to the magnetic field $B$ . The emf induced between the axle and the rim of the wheel
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952850/preptracker/questions/q_112361_explanation_1764952850522.png
Consider a single spoke in the wheel as shown in the figure.
Motional emf developed in the small element $dl$
$d\varepsilon = Bv\,dl$
$\varepsilon = \int d \varepsilon = \int_o^L B \omega ldl = B\omega \int_o^L l dl = \frac{1}{2}B\omega {L^2}$
Motional emf generated in each spoke is same. Since all the spokes are connected in parallel, total emf between axle and rim remains same.
subject: Physics

question: A train with an axle of length $1.66\,m$ is moving towards north with a speed of $90\,km{h^{ - 1}}$. If the vertical component of the earth's magnetic field is $0.2 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,T$, the emf induced across the ends of the axle of the train is
explanation:
Given
Length of axle $ = 1.66\,m$
Speed of train,
$v = 90\,km\,{h^{ - 1}} = \frac{{90 \times 1000}}{{60 \times 60}} = 25\,m/s$
Vertical component of magnetic field,
$B_{v}=0.2 \times 10^{-4} T$
$Emf$ induced, $c=B_{v} l v$
$=0.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.66 \times 25$
$ = 8.3 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,V = 0.83\,mV$
subject: Physics

question: Match the statements related to motional emf (Column I) with their corresponding expressions and outcomes (Column II). Choose the correct option from the given codes.
explanation:
A matches with R because when the conductor moves, it induces a current $I$ in the circuit.
B matches with S because the formula for magnetic flux ($\phi_{B}$) when the conductor moves with velocity $v$ is given by the product $B l v$.
C matches with Q because the work done in moving the charge across a magnetic field B with velocity v and length $l$ is $q v B l$.
D matches with P because the rate of change of the area enclosed by the circuit as the conductor moves is given by $\dfrac{d A}{d t}=l\left(\dfrac{d x}{d t}\right)$, which is equal to the negative of the velocity $l(-v)$.

options.text: A - Q,        	B - R,	C - S, 	D - P
A - R, 	B - S,	C - P, 	D - Q
A - R, 	B - P, 	C - S, 	D - Q
A - R,  	B - S, 	C - Q, 	D - P

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
An induced emf in a moving conductor can be calculated by considering the Lorentz force alone, without the need for an electric field.
The existence of an induced emf or current requires the presence of a time varying magnetic field which generates an electric field.
explanation: Statement  A  is false because while the Lorentz force is indeed responsible for the separation of charges that leads to an induced emf when a conductor moves through a magnetic field, this is not the only way emf can be induced. An electric field can also be induced in the absence of motion if there is a time varying magnetic field. Statement B is true because a time-varying magnetic field does induce an electric field, which is different in nature from the fields produced by static charges. This is a cornerstone of electromagnetic induction and essential to understanding the relationship between electricity and magnetism, as discovered by Faraday.  Correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The magnetic field's orientation has no impact on the induced motional emf in a conductor moving within it.
A circular loop and a rectangular loop moving out of a uniform magnetic field into a field-free region will both exhibit a constant induced emf if their velocities are constant.
explanation: Statement  A  is false because the orientation of the magnetic field relative to the conductor's motion is crucial for determining the presence of motional emf. The motional emf occurs only when there is a component of the conductor's velocity perpendicular to the magnetic field lines. Statement B is false because the induced emf in a circular loop would not remain constant during its exit from the magnetic field due to the non-uniform change in the area enclosed by the loop, while the induced emf in a rectangular loop could remain constant if the velocity is constant. Correct option is (4)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The motional emf induced in a conductor is not influenced by the direction of the magnetic field relative to the conductor's motion.
The direction of the induced emf is such that it tends to oppose the motion of the conductor that causes it.
explanation: Statement A is false because the direction of the magnetic field relative to the conductor's motion is crucial in determining the presence and direction of the motional emf. The motional  emf  occurs when the conductor moves perpendicularly to the magnetic field. Correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
A straight conductor moving in a time-independent magnetic field cannot generate an induced electromotive force (emf).
The induced emf in such a conductor is dependent on the velocity of the conductor, the length of the conductor within the magnetic field, and the strength of the magnetic field.
explanation: Statement  A  is false because a straight conductor moving with a constant velocity in a time-independent magnetic field can indeed generate an induced emf, known as motional emf. Statement B  is true, as the induced emf is given by the equation $\varepsilon=B l v$, where B is the magnetic field strength, $l$ is the length of the conductor, and $v$ is the velocity of the conductor. Correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
A conductor moving through a magnetic field does not always induce an emf.
The magnitude of the induced motional emf is given by the product of the magnetic field strength, the velocity of the conductor, and the length of the conductor within the magnetic field.
explanation: Statement  A  is false because a conductor moving through a magnetic field will induce an emf as long as there is a component of velocity perpendicular to the magnetic field. Statement B is true as the magnitude of the induced motional emf is indeed given by the product of the magnetic field strength (B), the length of the conductor ($l$), and the component of the conductor's velocity $(v)$ perpendicular to the magnetic field, as described by the equation $\varepsilon=B l v$. Correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: If a conductor moves in a direction parallel to a magnetic field, no emf will be induced in the conductor.

Reason: The Lorentz force acting on the charge carriers is zero when the motion of the conductor is parallel to the magnetic field.
explanation: The assertion is true because motional emf is generated when there is a component of velocity perpendicular to the magnetic field. When the conductor moves parallel to the magnetic field, this perpendicular component is zero, hence no emf is induced. The reason correctly explains that the Lorentz force, which is responsible for inducing emf, is indeed zero when the velocity of the conductor is parallel to the magnetic field. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: The induced electromotive force (emf) in a conductor moving in a magnetic field is called motional emf.

Reason: Motional emf is generated due to the Lorentz force acting on the free charge carriers in the conductor as it moves through the magnetic field.
explanation: The assertion correctly states the concept of motional emf as it pertains to a conductor moving within a magnetic field. The reason provided is correct as it explains the physical phenomenon - the Lorentz force - that results in the generation of motional emf. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

subject: Physics

question: If the vertical component of the earth's magnetic field is $0.5 \times 10^{-4} {~T}$ at a point. When an aeroplane of wing span $4 m$ is moving horizontally at this place at  $360\,\,km{h^{ - 1}},$  then the motional emf forced across the ends of the wings is
explanation:
Given,
$V_{\text {aeroplane }}=360 {~km} / {h}$ or $=100 {~m} / {s}$
Wing span, $l=4 {~m}$
Vertical component of
magnetic field of earth, $B_{V}=0.5 \times 10^{-4} T$
The induced emf is given by
$e=B l v$
Where $B$ is component of earth's magnetic field.
$l$ is length of wing span.
$v$ is speed of aeroplane
$e=0.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 4 \times 100=2 \times 10^{-2}$ or $20 \times 10^{-3} V$
Hence, value of emf is $20 \times 10^{-3} V$
Hence, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: An aeroplane is travelling horizontally towards west with a speed of $540 {kmh}^{-1}$. The wing span of the plane is  $20{\text{ }}m.$  If the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field at the location is $2.5 \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-4} {~T}$ and the dip angle is $30^{\circ}$, the potential difference developed between the ends of the wing is
explanation:
Speed of aeroplane,
$v=540 {~km} / {h}=540 \times \dfrac{5}{18} {~m} / {s}=150 {~m} / {s}$
Length of wing span, $l=20 {~m}$
$B_{H}=25 \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-4} T$
$\delta=30^{\circ}$
Potential difference developed between the ends of the wing
$\varepsilon=B_{V} v l=\left(B_{H} \tan \delta\right) v l$
$=2.5 \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-4} \times \tan 30^{\circ} \times 150 \times 20$
$=2.5 \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-4} \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \times 3 \times 10^{3}=0.75 {~V}$
Hence, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A wheel of 20 metallic spokes each  $40{\text{ }}cm$  long is rotated with a speed of  $180\,\,rev/\min $  in a plane normal to the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field $B_{H}$ at a place. If $B_{H}=0.4 G$ (Gauss) at that place, the induced emf between the axle and the rim of the wheel is
explanation:
Given, length of each spokes of wheel,
$l=40 {~cm}=0.4 {~m}$
Angular velocity,
$\omega=2 \pi f=2 \pi \times \dfrac{180}{60} {rad} / {s}=6 \pi {rad} / {s}$
Horizontal component of earth's magnetic field,
$B_{H}=0.4 G=0.4 \times 10^{-4} T$
$\therefore$ Induced emf, $e=\dfrac{1}{2} B \omega L^{2}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 6 \pi \times(0.4)^{2}$
$=1.2 \pi \times 10^{-4} \times 0.16$
$=0.192 \pi \times 10^{-4} V$
$=192 \pi \times 10^{-7} V$
subject: Physics

question: A long straight solenoid with cross-sectional radius ' $a$ ' and number of turns per unit length $n$ has a current varying with time as $I\left(A s^{-1}\right)$. The magnitude of the electric field as a function of distance $r$ from the solenoid axis is
explanation:
Given, radius of solenoid be $a$, Number of turns per unit length be $n$,
Current per unit time $I=\dfrac{i}{t}$,
and distance from axis of solenoid $r$.
Let, electric field be $E$.
Since, $\oint E \cdot d l=-\dfrac{d \phi}{d t}$
$E \cdot 2 \pi r=\dfrac{B \cdot A}{t}=\dfrac{n \mu_{0} i \cdot \pi a^{2}}{t}$
$\Rightarrow E=\dfrac{n \mu_{0} I a^{2}}{2 r}$
subject: Physics

question: A boy is playing with the empty rim of a cycle wheel of radius  $40{\text{ }}cm$  by rolling it along a horizontal road towards north with angular speed of $20 {rads}^{-1}$. Considering the effect of magnetic field of earth,   the   e.m.f   induced in the rim is (Horizontal component of earth's magnetic field $=0.26 G$)
explanation:
Given, Radius of rim, $r=40 {~cm}=0.4 {~m}$
Angular speed, $\omega = 20\,\,rad/s$
Horizontal component of earth's magnetic field,
$B = 0.26\,\,G = 0.26 \times {10^{ - 4}}T$
The formula used for induced emf
$e=$ Work done per unit charge
$e=l(v \times B)$
As plane of wheel's revolution is parallel to earth's horizontal component of magnetic field, therefore induced emf is zero. i. e. , $e=0$
subject: Physics

question: A conducting rod is moving towards right with a velocity $v$ in a uniform magnetic field $B$. If the direction of induced current $i$ is as shown in the figure, then the direction of $B$ is
explanation:
Given, Velocity of conducting rod $=v$
Magnetic field $=B$
Induced current $=i$
For induced current direction, we use Fleming's right hand rule.
It states that if thumb, fore finger and middle finger of right hand are mutually perpendicular to each other, then if fore finger indicate the direction of external magnetic field $(B)$ and thumb shows direction of motion of conductor, then middle finger will show the direction of induced current and hence direction of emf.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952849/preptracker/questions/q_112291_explanation_1764952848999.png
Hence, from diagram and applying the Fleming's right hand rule, the direction of magnetic field is perpendicular to plane of paper and into the paper.
Hence, the correct option is (3).

options.text: in the plane of the paper towards right.
in the plane of the paper towards left.
perpendicular to the plane of the paper and into the paper.
perpendicular to the plane of the paper and out of the paper.

subject: Physics

question: A metal disc of radius  $30{\text{ }}cm$  rotates with a constant angular velocity $\omega=100 {rad} / {s}$ about its axis. Find the magnitude of potential difference between the centre and the rim of the disc in the external uniform magnetic field   of magnitude, $B=4 m T$ is directed perpendicular to the disc.
explanation:
Radius of metal disc,
$R=30 {~cm}=0.3 {~m}$,
Angular velocity, $\omega = 100\,\,rad/s,$
Magnetic field, $B=4 m T=4 \times 10^{-3} T$
Magnitude of potential difference between the
centre and the rim of the disc,
$e=\dfrac{1}{2} B \omega R^{2}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 \times(0.3)^{2}$
$=2 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 \times 0.09$
$=18 \times 10^{-3} {~V}=18 {mV}$
Hence, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: An infinitely long cylinder is kept parallel to a uniform magnetic field $B$ directed along the positive $Z$ - axis. The direction of induced current as seen from the $Z$ - axis will be
explanation:
Given,
Direction of current $i$ and magnetic field $B$ is same, $\,\,$ $\therefore \theta=0^{\circ}$
Now, As we know that
$e=-\dfrac{d \phi}{d t}=-B N A \omega \sin \theta$
where, $e=$ induced e.m.f
$\phi=$ flux
$N=$ number of turns
$A=$ Area,
and $\omega=$ angular frequency.
$\therefore \quad e=B N \cdot A \omega \sin 0^{\circ}=0 V$
Hence, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: When a metal road of length $l$ is placed in a uniform magnetic field $B$ and moved with velocity $v$ perpendicular to the field, then the induced emf across its ends is
explanation:
The given situation is shown below
$\therefore$ The distance covered by the rod at time $d t$ is $v d t$.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958016/preptracker/questions/q_112286_explanation_1764958015975.png
$\therefore$ The area covered by the $\operatorname{rod} A=v d t \times l$
In time $d t$, magnetic flux linked with the rod, is $d \phi=B \times A=B \times(v d t \times l)=B(v \times l) d t$
$\therefore $ emf induced, $e=\dfrac{d \phi}{d t}=-B \times(v \times l)$
$=B(l \times v)=B l v \sin \phi$
$=B v l \quad\left[\because \theta=90^{\circ} \Rightarrow \sin 90^{\circ}=1\right]$
Hence, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: Two straight conducting rails form a right angle as shown below. A conducting bar in contact with the rails starts at the vertex at time, $t=0$ and moves with a constant velocity of $v=5 {~m} / {s}$ along them. A magnetic field with $B=0.1 T$ is directed out of the page. The absolute value of the emf around the triangle at the time $t=4 s$ will be
explanation:
The given situation is shown below
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952846/preptracker/questions/q_112285_explanation_1764952845607.png
For triangle $P Q R, x=v \times t$
So, at $t=4 {~s}, x=5 \times 4=20 {~m}$
Now in $\triangle P R S$,
$\dfrac{S R}{S P}=\tan 45^{\circ} \Rightarrow \quad S R=S P \quad\left(\because \tan 45^{\circ}=1\right)$
or length $P Q$ of rod is $2 \times S R=2 x=40 {~m}$
i. e. , area swept in $4 s=$ area $\triangle P Q R$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \times$ base $\times$ height
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 2 x \times x=x^{2}=(20)^{2}=400 m^{2}$
Area swept in $1 s=\dfrac{d A}{d t}=\dfrac{400}{4}=100 {~m}^{2} / {s}$
So, magnitude of emf induced in triangular loop is
$e=\left|\dfrac{d \phi}{d t}\right|=B\left(\dfrac{d A}{d t}\right)=0.1 \times 100=10 {~V}$
Hence, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A metal disc of radius, $a=10 {~cm}$ rotates with a constant angular speed of  $\omega  = 200\,\,rad/s$  about its axis. The potential difference between the centre and the rim of the disc under a uniform magnetic field, $B=5 m T$ directed perpendicular to the disc, is
explanation:
Key Idea Induced emf due to a rotating metallic disc in uniform magnetic field $B$ is given by expression,
$e=\dfrac{1}{2} B \omega a^{2}$
where, $\omega=$ angular speed and $a=$ radius of the disc, $B=$ magnetic field
Given, $a=10 {~cm}$,
$\omega = 200\,\,rad/\sec $ and magnetic field
$B=5 m T$
Now, putting the values in above expression,
$e=\dfrac{1}{2} B \omega a^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow e=5 {mV}$
Hence, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A conducting wire has length $L$ is bent in the shape of semicircle and moves in its plane with constant velocity $v$. A uniform magnetic field $B$ exists in the direction perpendicular to the plane of the wire. The velocity makes an angle $45^{\circ}$ to the diameter joining free ends and the emf induced between the ends of the wire is $e=\alpha(B v L)$. The value of constant $\alpha$ is
explanation:
A semicircle of length $L$, moving with velocity $v$ in a uniform magnetic field is shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952840/preptracker/questions/q_112277_explanation_1764952839578.png
As, we know that the induced emf in a conductor of length $l, e=l(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$
$ = Bvl\sin \theta \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Since, we have semi-circular arc in this problem. So, the effective length of arc for induced emf,
${l_{eff}} = 2R = \frac{{2L}}{\pi }\quad (\because L = \pi R)\quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$e=B v \dfrac{2 L}{\pi} \sin 45^{\circ}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad e = BvL\frac{{\sqrt 2 }}{\pi }\left( {\because \theta = 45^\circ } \right)\quad \quad \quad \quad (3)$
Now, given induced emf, $e = \alpha BvL\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (4)$
Hence, from Eqs. (3) and (4), we get
$\alpha=\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\pi}$
Hence, option (4) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: A rod of length  $80{\text{ }}cm$  rotates about its midpoint with a frequency of  $10\,\,rev/s.$ The potential difference (in volts) between two ends of the rod due to a magnetic field, $B=0.5 T$ directed perpendicular to the rod is
explanation:
Given, frequency of revolution of the rod,
$f = 10\,\,rev/s,$
length of rod,
$L=80 {~cm}=0.8 {~m}$
and magnetic field $B=0.5 T$. Since, rod is rotated about its mid point, hence radius of circular revolution,
$r=\dfrac{L}{2}=\dfrac{0.8}{2}=0.4 {~m}$
$\therefore$ Potential difference between centre of rod and one end of rod due to revolution in magnetic is given by
$e=$ induced emf $=\dfrac{\text { change in flux }}{\text { time taken }}$
$e=\dfrac{B \cdot \pi r^{2}}{T}=B \cdot \pi r^{2} \cdot f$
$\left[\because f=\dfrac{l}{T}\right]$
$=0.5 \times \pi \times(0.4)^{2} \times 10=0.8 \pi V$
$\therefore$ Potential difference between two ends of rod due to revolution in the magnetic field,
$V=2 e=2 \times 0.8 \pi=1.6 \pi V$
Hence, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A wire loop enclosing a semi-circle of radius $R$ is located on the boundary of a uniform magnetic field of induction $B$. At time $t=0$, the loop is set into rotation with angular velocity angular $\omega$ about its axis $O$, coinciding with a line vector $B$ on the boundary as shown in the figure. The emf induced in the loop is
explanation:
By using Faraday Law of E. M. I., we have
$|e|=\dfrac{d}{d t} \phi_{B}=\left(\dfrac{d}{d t} A\right) \cdot B$
$=\left(\dfrac{\pi R^{2}}{2 \pi / \omega}\right) B=\dfrac{1}{2} B R^{2} \omega$
Hence, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A conducting rod of length $L$ lies in $X Y$-plane and makes an angle $30^{\circ}$ with $X$-axis. One end of the rod lies at origin initially. A magnetic field also exists in the region pointing along positive $Z$-direction. The magnitude of the magnetic field varies with $y$ as $B_{0}\left(\dfrac{y}{L}\right)^{3}$, where, $B_{0}$ is a constant. At some instant the rod starts moving with a velocity $v_{0}$ along $X$-axis. The emf induced in the rod is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952843/preptracker/questions/q_112272_explanation_1764952842830.png
Length along $Y$-axis would be $L \sin 30^{\circ}=\dfrac{L}{2}$
length $=L$
Velocity of rod $=v_{0}$, along $X$-axis
variable magnetic field, $B=B_{0}\left(\dfrac{y}{L}\right)^{3}$, along $Z$ axis.
Now, induced emf (formula)
$e=B l v$
where, $B, l$ and $v$ are perpendicular to each other, $l=L / 2$ Now, consider a small element of length $d y$ at a distance $y$ from origin along $Y$-axis, then emf of element
$d e=B(d y) v_{0}=B_{0} \dfrac{y^{3}}{L^{3}} v_{0} \cdot d y$
Emf of the rod,
$e=\dfrac{B_{0} v_{0}}{L^{3}} \int_{0}^{L / 2} y^{3} \cdot d y=\dfrac{B_{0} v_{0}}{L^{3}}\left[\dfrac{y^{4}}{4}\right]^{L / 2}$
$e=\dfrac{B_{0} v_{0}}{L^{3}}\left[\dfrac{(L / 2)^{4}}{4}\right] \Rightarrow e=\dfrac{B_{0} v_{0} L}{64}$
Hence, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A wheel with radial metal spokes $1 m$ in length is rotated in a magnetic field of $0.5 \times 10^{-4} {~T}$ normal to the plane of the wheel. If the induced emf between the rim and axle is $\dfrac{\pi}{3000} V$, then the rotational speed of the wheel in revolutions per minute is
explanation:
Induced emf between rim and axle of wheel,
$e=\dfrac{1}{2} B \omega R^{2}$
So, $\quad \dfrac{\pi}{3000}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-4} \times \omega \times 1^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \omega=\dfrac{2 \times \pi}{3000 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-4}}$
$2 \pi f=\dfrac{2 \pi \times 10000 \times 2}{3000}$
$\Rightarrow$ Rotation frequency in revolutions per minute
$f=\dfrac{10000 \times 2}{3000} \times 60=400 {rpm}$
Hence, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A square loop of area $25\,c{m^2}$ has a resistance of $10 \Omega$. The loop is placed in uniform magnetic field of magnitude $40.0\,T.$ The plane of loop is perpendicular to the magnetic field. The work done in pulling the loop out of the magnetic field slowly and uniformly in $1.0\,\sec ,$ will be
explanation:
Given
$A = 25\,c{m^2},R = 10\Omega ,B = 40T,t = 1\,s$
Let the length of side is $l$.
$A = {l^2} \Rightarrow 25 = {l^2} \Rightarrow l = 5\,cm$
so, $v = \frac{l}{t} = \frac{{0.05}}{1} = 0.05\,m/s$
Voltage, $V = Bvl = 40 \times 0.05 \times 0.05 = 0.1\,V$
Current, $i = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{{0.1}}{{10}} = 0.01\,A$
Force $=\operatorname{Bil} \times \sin 90^{\circ}$
Work $=$ Force distance
$=40 \times 0.01 \times 0.05 \times 0.05$
$W = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Energy Changes in Case of Motional EMF

question: A square loop of side $15\,cm$ being moved towards right at a constant speed of $2\,cm/s$ as shown in figure. The front edge enters the $50\,cm$ wide magnetic field at $t=0$. The value of induced emf in the loop at $t = 10\,s$ will be
explanation:
Speed $ = 2\,cm/s$
Đistance $ = 50\,cm$
Time taken to cover magnetic field,
$t = \frac{{50}}{2} = 25\,s$
So, the loop is completely in magnetic field, no flux change and hence no induced emf.
As per Faraday's law, $\varepsilon=\dfrac{-d \phi}{d t}$
$\therefore \varepsilon=0$. Correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: In figure, there are two sliders and they can slide on two frictionless parallel wires in uniform magnetic field ${B}$, which is present everywhere. The mass of each slider is ${m}$, resistance ${R}$ and initially these are at rest. Now, if one slider is given a velocity ${v_{0}=16 {~m} {~s}^{-1}}$, what will be the velocity   of other slider after long time? (neglect the self-induction)
explanation:
After long time, from conservation of momentum.
$m v_{0}=2 m v ; v=\dfrac{v_{0}}{2}=8 {~m} {~s}^{-1}$
subject: Physics

question: A pair of long conducting rails are held vertical at a separation ${l=50 {~cm}}$. The top ends are connected by a resistance of ${R=0.5 \Omega}$ and a fuse ${(F)}$ of negligible resistance. A conducting rod is free to slide along the rails under gravity The whole system is in a uniform horizontal magnetic field ${B=1.5 {~T}}$ as shown. Resistance of the rod and rails are negligible and the rod remains horizontal as it moves. The rod is released from rest. Find the minimum mass of the rod  that will ensure that the fuse blows out. It is known that rating of the fuse is 4 $A$ .
explanation:
When current in the circuit is $I=4 {~A}$, magnetic force on the rod is
$F=I l B=4 \times 0.5 \times 1.5=3 {~N}$
The weight of the rod must exceed $F$ to blow out the fuse.
$\begin{aligned}m g & \geq 3 \\\therefore \quad m & \geq \dfrac{3}{10}=0.3 {~kg}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question:
The long, horizontal pair of rails shown in the figure is connected using resistance ${R}$. The distance between the rails is ${l}$, the electrical resistance of the rails is negligible. A conducting wire of mass ${m}$ and length ${l}$ can slide without friction on the pair of rails,in a vertical, homogeneous magnetic field of induction $B$. A force of magnitude ${F_{0}}$ is exerted for sufficiently long time onto the conducting wire, so that the speed of the wire becomes nearly constant. The force ${F_{0}}$ is now removed wire certain point ${P}$. What distance does the conducting wire cover on rails from point ${P}$ before stopping ? (Given: ${F_0} = 20\,N,m = 1.0\,g,R = 0.01\Omega ,$
$l = 10\,cm,B = 0.1\,T$ )
explanation:
For constant velocity, acceleration of the wire will be zero
$F_{0}=F_{m}=i l B=\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon}{B}\right) l B=\left(\dfrac{B l v_{0}}{R}\right) l B$
$v_{0}=\dfrac{F_{0} R}{B^{2} l^{2}}$ gives velocity at point $P$
Now, retardation $a=\dfrac{F_{m}}{m}=\dfrac{i l B}{m}$
$a=\dfrac{B^{2} l^{2}}{m R} v \Rightarrow-v \dfrac{d v}{d s}=\dfrac{B^{2} l^{2}}{m R} v$$-\int_{v_{0}}^{0} d v=\dfrac{B^{2} l^{2}}{m R} \int_{0}^{s} d s \Rightarrow v_{0}=\dfrac{B^{2} l^{2}}{m R} s$
$s=\dfrac{m R v_{0}}{B^{2} l^{2}}=\dfrac{F_{0} m R^{2}}{B^{4} l^{4}}=200 {~m}$
subject: Physics

question: Wire ${P Q}$ with negligible resistance slides on the three rails with ${5 {~cm} / {sec}}$. If the current in ${10\,\, \Omega}$ resistance when switch ${S}$ is connected to position 1 is ${i_{1}}$ and when the switch is at position 2 the current becomes ${i_{2}}$. Find the ratio ${\dfrac{i_{1}}{i_{2}}}$.
explanation:
For position 1, Induced current ${i_1} = \frac{e}{R} = \frac{{Bvl}}{R}$
${i_1} = \frac{{1 \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}} \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{10}} = 0.1\,mA$
For position 2, Induced current
${i_2} = \frac{e}{R} = \frac{{Bv(2l)}}{R}$
${i_2}= \frac{{1 \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}} \times 4 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{10}} = 0.2\,mA$
$\therefore \frac{{{i_1}}}{{{i_2}}} = \frac{{0.1}}{{0.2}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.50$
subject: Physics

question: A structure formed from two straight conducting rods forming a right angle, is placed in a uniform magnetic field with ${B=0.40 {~T}}$ is directed out of the page. A straight conducting bar in contact with the structure, starts at the vertex at time ${t=0}$ and moves with a constant velocity of ${5.0 {~m} / {s}}$ along them. The $emf$ around the triangle at that time ${t=4.0 {~s}}$ is
explanation:
The perpendicular length of the triangular area enclosed by the structure and bar is the same as the distance travelled by it in time ${t: d=v t}$, where ${v=5.0 {~m} / {s}}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950749/preptracker/questions/q_44015_explanation_1764950748803.png Here the "base" of that triangle (the distance between the intersection points of the bar with the structure) is ${2 d}$. Thus, the area of the triangle is
$A=\dfrac{1}{2}(\text { base })(\text { height })=\dfrac{1}{2}(2 v t)(v t)=v^{2} t^{2}$
Since the field is a uniform ${B=0.350 {~T}}$, then the magnitude of the flux (in $SI$ units) is
$\phi_{B}=B A=(0.40)(5.0)^{2} t^{2}=10.0 t^{2}$
The magnitude of the emf is the (absolute value of) Faraday's law:
$\varepsilon = \frac{{d{\Phi _B}}}{{dt}} = 10.0\frac{{d{t^2}}}{{dt}} = 20.0\,t$
At ${t=4.0 {~s}}$, this yields ${\varepsilon=80.0 {~V}}$.
subject: Physics

question: An aircraft with a wing span of $40\,m$ flies with a speed of $1080\,km{h^{ - 1}}$ in the eastward direction at a constant altitude in the northern hemisphere, where the vertical component of earth's magnetic field is $1.75 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,T.$ The $emf$   that develops between the tips of the wing is
explanation:
The metallic part between the wing-tips can be treated as a single conductor cutting flux-lines due to vertical component of earth's magnetic field. So emf is induced between the tips of its wings. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950743/preptracker/questions/q_44007_explanation_1764950742442.png $l = 40\,m,{B_v} = 1.75 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,T,v = 1080\,km{h^{ - 1}}$
$ = \frac{{1080 \times 1000}}{{3600}}\,m{s^{ - 1}} = 300\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore \varepsilon = {B_V}lv = 1.75 \times {10^{ - 5}} \times 40 \times 300 = 0.21\,V$
subject: Physics

question: A pair of parallel horizontal conducting rails of negligible resistance shorted at one end is fixed on a table. The distance between the rails is ${l}$. A conducting massless rod of resistance ${R}$ can slide on the rails frictionlessly. The rod is tied to a massless string which passes over a pulley fixed to the edge of the table. A mass ${m}$, tied to the other end of the string, hangs vertically. A constant magnetic field ${B}$ exists perpendicular to the table. If the system is released from rest, calculate the acceleration of the mass at the instant when the velocity of the rod is half the terminal velocity
explanation:
If $v$ is the velocity of the rod at any instant, the $emf$ induced in it will be $Bvl.$ And as the resistance of the rod is $R,$ the induced current will be
$I=\dfrac{e}{R}=\dfrac{B v l}{R}$
Due to this induced current a force will act on the rod which will oppose its motion.
${F_M} = BIl = \frac{{{B^2}{l^2}v}}{R}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950740/preptracker/questions/q_43987_explanation_1764950740518.png So if ${T}$ is the tension in the string, equation of motion of rod and mass ${m}$ will be
$T-F_{M}=0 \times a \text { and } m g-T=m a$
So eliminating ${T}$ between these
$mg - {F_M} = ma\,\,i.e.,a = g - \frac{{{F_M}}}{m}$
and on substituting the value of ${F_{M}}$ from Eq. (1)
$a=g-\dfrac{B^{2} l^{2}}{m R} v$
$i.e.,$ ${\quad a=g-\dfrac{v}{t}}$ with $\tau = \frac{{mR}}{{{{(Bl)}^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
So the rod will achieve terminal velocity ${v_{T}}$ when ${a=0}$,
$i.e.,$ ${\quad g-\dfrac{v_{T}}{\tau}=0}$
$i.e.,$ ${v_T} = \tau g = \frac{{mgR}}{{{{(Bl)}^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
and so for ${v=\dfrac{1}{2} v_{T}=\dfrac{1}{2} \tau g}$, from Eq. (2),
$a=g-\dfrac{1}{\tau}\left(\dfrac{1}{2} \tau g\right)=\dfrac{1}{2} g=5 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A metal disc of radius ${R}=10 {~cm}$ rotates with a constant angular velocity $\omega=100\, {rad} / {s}$ about its axis. When the external magnetic field is absent, the potential difference between the centre and rim of the disc is found to be $2.84 \times 10^{-{x}} {V}$.   Find  the value of $x$
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950693/preptracker/questions/q_43939_explanation_1764950690137.png
Centripetal force required for circular motion of electron is generated by a radial electric field caused by the redistribution of the electrons in the disc.
${F}_{{c}}={f}_{{e}}$
$\Rightarrow {mr} \omega^{2}={eE}$
$\Rightarrow {E}=\dfrac{{mr} \omega^{2}}{{e}}$
Now potential difference between the rim and the centre of disc is,
${V}_{{R}}-{V}_{{C}}=\Delta {V}=\int_{0}^{{d}} {Edr}$
$\therefore \quad \Delta {V}=\dfrac{-{m} \omega^{2}}{{e}} \int_{0}^{{R}} {rdr}$
$=\dfrac{m \omega^{2} R^{2}}{2 {e}}=\dfrac{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31}\right)(100)^{2}(0.1)^{2}}{2 \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)}$
$\therefore \quad \Delta {V}=2.84 \times 10^{-10} {~V}$
subject: Physics

question:
The figure shows a square loop ${L}$ of side $5 {~cm}$ which is connected to a network of resistances. The whole setup is moving towards right with a constant speed of $1 {cms}^{-1}$. At some instant, a part of $L$ is in a uniform magnetic field of $1 T$, perpendicular to the plane of loop. If the resistance of $L$ is $2.7 \,\Omega$, the current in the loop at that instant is found out to be ${N} \times 10^{-4} {~A}$
$\text { Find N. }\left[\text { Take } \dfrac{4}{3}=1.3\right]$
explanation:
$3\Omega $ resistances can be neglected as, the given combination of resistances form balanced wheat stone bridge.
Req $=\dfrac{4 \times 2}{4+2}=\dfrac{4}{3} \Omega$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950686/preptracker/questions/q_43915_explanation_1764950686135.png Total resistance, ${R}=1.3+2.7=4 \Omega$
emf induced, $\varepsilon={B} / {v}$$=1 \times 5 \times 10^{-2} \times 1 \times 10^{-2}$
$\therefore \varepsilon=5 \times 10^{-4} {~V}$
$\therefore \quad {i}_{\text {induced }}=\dfrac{\varepsilon}{{R}}=\dfrac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{4}=1.25 \times 10^{-4} {~A}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Energy Changes in Case of Motional EMF

question: The loop shown moves with a velocity $v$ in a uniform magnetic field of magnitude $B$, directed into the paper. The potential difference between $P$ and $Q$ is $e$. Then :
explanation:
The Potential difference across $P$ and $Q$ is
$e = Blv$
where $l$ is the effect length of $PQ$ which is
$l = \frac{L}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow e = \frac{{BLv}}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A conducting rod ${A B}$ of length ${l=1 {~m}}$ is moving at a velocity ${v=4 {~m} / {s}}$, making an angle ${30^{\circ}}$ with its length. If a uniform magnetic field ${B=2 T}$ exists in a direction perpendicular to the plane of motion, then
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956496/preptracker/questions/q_43803_explanation_1764956496343.png ${=B v_{\perp} l=B(v \sin \theta) l=2 \times 4 \sin 30 \times 1}$
${\therefore \quad V_{A}-V_{B}=4 V}$
subject: Physics

question: An $emf$ of $0.08\,V$ is induced in a metal rod of length $10\,cm$ held normal to a uniform magnetic field of $0.4 T$, when moves with a velocity of
explanation:
Motional $Emf,$ $e = 0.08\,V$, length $l = 10\,cm = 0.1\,m$
Magnetic field, $B=0.4 T$
As rod is held perpendicular to the magnetic field, so $\theta=90^{\circ}$ In a metal rod, induced $emf$ is given by, $e=(\vec{V} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \vec{l}$
When $\vec{V}, \vec{B}$ and $\vec{l}$ are mutually perpendicular, where $v$ is velocity of moving rod
or $v=\dfrac{e}{B l}=\dfrac{0.08}{0.4 \times 0.1}$
$\therefore v = \frac{{0.08}}{{0.04}} = 2\,m/s$
subject: Physics

question: A wire of length $1\,m$ moving with velocity $8\,m/s$ at right angles to a magnetic field of $2 T$. The magnitude of induced emf, between the ends of wire will be
explanation:
Here, $l=1 m, v=8 m / s, \theta=90^{\circ}, B=2 T$ Induced $emf,$
$e = (\vec V \times \vec B) \cdot \vec l = Bvl\sin \theta $
$ = 2 \times 8 \times 1 \times 1 = 16V$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: On moving a straight wire of copper in a uniform magnetic field cutting the lines of force, an $emf$ is induced between the ends of the wire.

Reason: The Lorentz force acts on the free electrons of copper wire when moved in magnetic field.
explanation: Assertion is correct by Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction. The Lorentz force $\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ is the mechanism behind this induction, acting on the free electrons within the copper wire as it moves in the magnetic field. Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Situations of induced electric field due to changing magnetic flux are more readily observed than situations of induced magnetic field due to changing electric flux.

Reason: The phenomenon of induction of either field is not linked to flux.
explanation: Induced electric fields due to changing magnetic flux are more readily observed and are a common occurrence. It is aspect of electromagnetic induction, described by Faraday's law. Induced magnetic fields due to changing electric fields also exist but are less commonly observed. The induced field depends on changes in flux, is also accurate. In both cases, the rate of change of one field (electric or magnetic) results in the induction of the other field. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Time dependent magnetic field generates electric field.

Reason: Direction of electric field generated from time variable magnetic field does not obey Lenz's law.
explanation:
Magnetic flux $\phi=\vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}$
For there to be induced electric field, either magnetic field $\vec{B}$ or 'Area' shall change with time. $B$ is given as function of $t$. Then,$\left(A \dfrac{d B}{d t}\right)$ gives $\left(\dfrac{d \phi}{d t}\right)$ which is essential for induced emf given by
$V_{\text {ind }}=\dfrac{-d \phi}{d t}=\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}$
In above negative sign is attributed to Lenz's law Assertion is correct but reason is not correct. So, correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The work done by a charge in a closed loop of induced current is non-zero.

Reason: Induced electric field is non-conservative in nature.
explanation:
For induced electric field $\vec{E}$,
$\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=-\dfrac{d \phi}{d t} \neq 0$
$\Rightarrow$ non - zero work is done
For conservation field (or force $F$), we can define a potential energy $(t)$ as a function of distance $\mathrm{r}$ such that $F=\dfrac{-d U}{d r}$
In case of 'induced electric field' such function $U$ cannot be defined
$\Rightarrow$ Induced electric field is non - conservative
$\Rightarrow$ Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is correct explanation for AssertionSo, correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The induced emf in a conducting loop of wire will be non zero when it rotates in a uniform magnetic field.

Reason: There is change in magnetic flux in this scenario.
explanation: Due to rotational motion between loop and $\vec{B}$, there is change of flux as a function of time. $EMF$ is induced. Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A cycle wheel with 64 spokes is rotating with $N$ rotations per second at right angles to horizontal component of magnetic field. The induced e.m.f. generated between its axle and rim is $E$. If the number of spokes is reduced to 32 then the value of induced e.m.f. will be
explanation: E.M.F. is independent of no of spokes here. The emf induced across each spoke would be same and it can be imagined as through they all are in parallel.
subject: Physics

question: A conducting loop being pulled out of magnetic field with a speed $v$. Which of the following plots may represent the power delivered by the pulling agent as a function of the speed $v$ ?
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957145/preptracker/questions/q_43550_explanation_1764957144827.png Induced $emf = Bvl$
Induced current, $i=\dfrac{B v l}{R}$
Magnet force on loop, $F_{m}=B i l=\dfrac{B^{2} v l^{2}}{R}$
Since, loop is moving with constant speed.
$\Rightarrow F=F_{m}$
Input power, $P=F \cdot v=F v=\dfrac{B^{2} v^{2} l^{2}}{R}$
$P$ versus $v$ graph will be like,
$y \propto x^{2}$ (parabola, open upward).
subject: Physics

question: A metal conductor of length $2\,m$ rotates vertically about one of its ends at an angular velocity of $50\,rad{\rm{/}}\sec $. If the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field is $0.5\,T$, then the $E M F$ developed between the two ends of conductor.
explanation:
$Emf$ induced,
$E = \frac{1}{2}B{l^2}\omega $
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.5 \times 4 \times 50 = 50\,V$
subject: Physics

question: A rod of length $\ell$ rotates with a uniform angular velocity $\omega$ about an axis passing through its middle point but normal to its length in a uniform magnetic field of induction $B$ with its direction parallel to the axis of rotation. The induced $E M F$ between the two ends of the rod is:
explanation:
Length of the rod between the axis of rotation and one end of the $\operatorname{rod}=\dfrac{\ell}{2}$.
Area swept out in one rotation
$=\pi\left(\dfrac{\ell}{2}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{\pi \ell^{2}}{4}\right)$
Angular velocity $=\omega \mathrm{rad} \mathrm{s}{ }^{-1}$.
Frequency of revolution $=\dfrac{\omega}{2 \pi}$, Area swept out per second $=\dfrac{\pi \ell^{2}}{4}\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2 \pi}\right)=\dfrac{\ell^{2} \omega}{8}$,
Magnetic induction $=B$.
Rate of change of magnetic flux $=\left(\dfrac{B \ell^{2} \omega}{8}\right)$,
Magnitude of induced emf $=\dfrac{B \ell^{2} \omega}{8}$
Magnitude of induced $E M F$ between the axis and the other end is also $\left(\dfrac{B \ell^{2} \omega}{8}\right)$. These two $E M F$ 's are in the opposite directions. Hence, the potential difference between the two ends of the rod is zero.
subject: Physics

question: A wire of length $50\,cm$ moves with a velocity of $300\,m{\rm{/}}\min $, perpendicular to a magnetic field. If the e.m.f induced in the wire is $2\,V$, the magnitude of the field in tesla is
explanation:
The magnitude of induced e.m. $f$ is given by
$|{V_{ind}}| = Blv$
$v = 300\,m{\rm{/}}\min = 5\,m{\rm{/}}s$
$\therefore B = \frac{{|{V_{ind}}|}}{{lv}} = \frac{2}{{0.5 \times 5}} = 0.8$ tesla
subject: Physics

question: A square loop of side \[2\;cm\] enters a magnetic field with a constant speed of \[2\;cm\;{s^{ - 1}}\] as shown. The front edge enters the field at\[t = 0\,{\rm{sec}}\]. Which of the following graph correctly depicts the induced \[emf\] in the loop? (Take clockwise direction positive)
explanation:
${V_{{\rm{induced }}}} = Bvl = 2 \times {10^{ - 4}}Volt{\rm{ }} = e$
Due to Lenz's law, the graph starts at $\left( { - 2 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}} \right)Volt$ .
Correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A metallic rod of length $1\,m$ held along east west direction is allowed to fall down freely. Given horizontal component of earth's magnetic field ${B_H} = 3 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,T$. The $emf$ induced in the rod at an instant $t=2 s$ after it is released is (Take $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ ).
explanation:
${B_H} = 3 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,T$
$u=0$
$ \Rightarrow u = gt = 10 \times 2 = 20\,m/\sec $
$\Rightarrow V_{\text {induced }}=B_{H} l v$
$ = 3 \times {10^{ - 5}} \times 1 \times 20 = 6 \times {10^{ - 4}}{\rm{ }}Volts{\rm{ }}.$
Correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A conducting square loop of side length ' $$d$$ ' with its edges parallel to $x$-axis and $y$-axis move with velocity  ${v_0}\widehat i$ in a region having magnetic field $\overrightarrow B  = \frac{{{B_0}x}}{a}\widehat k$. Find the induced emf.
explanation:
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_08_16_d332cf96a937e125c97ag-7.jpg?height=330&width=349&top_left_y=2193&top_left_x=415
As the two sides parallel to $x$ axis are also parallel to velocity, no emf will be generated in those two sides. Induced emf will be generated only in the two sides which are parallel to $y$ axis as they are also perpendicular to velocity.
Using $\varepsilon = B{v_0}\,d$ (as $$\mathrm{B}, v_{0}$$ and $$\mathrm{d}$$ are perpendicular to each other):
${\varepsilon _1} = \frac{{{B_0}(x + d)}}{a}{v_0}\,d$
${\varepsilon _2} = \frac{{{B_0}x}}{a}{v_0}\,d$
${\varepsilon _{net{\text{ }}}} = {\varepsilon _1} - {\varepsilon _2} = \frac{{{B_0}{v_0}\,{d^2}}}{a}$
subject: Physics
