question: A long straight wire carrying current $i$ and $a$ rectangular frame with side lengths $a$ and $b$ lie in the same plane as shown in the figure. The mutual inductance of the wire and frame is
explanation:
Magnetic field through the wire,
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i b}{2 \pi a}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958018/preptracker/questions/q_112301_explanation_1764958017989.png
$\therefore$ Magnetic flux induced, $\phi=\oint B \cdot d a$
$\Rightarrow \phi=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i b}{2 \pi} \int_{a}^{a+a} \dfrac{d a}{a}$
$\Rightarrow \phi=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i b}{2 \pi} \ln 2$
$\therefore$ Mutual inductance of the wire and the frame
is $M=\dfrac{\phi}{i}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} b}{2 \pi} \ln 2$
subject: Physics

question: When a current $i$ through a solenoid is increasing at a constant rate, then the induced current is
explanation:
Current flowing through solenoid is increasing at constant rate.
Magnetic field associated with solenoid
$B=\mu_{0} n i$
$\dfrac{d B}{d t}=\mu_{0} n \dfrac{d i}{d t}$
as $d i / d t$ is constant, $d B / d t$ is also constant. Flux is given by
$\phi=B \cdot A$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{d \phi}{d t}=A \dfrac{d B}{d t} \quad(A$ is area $)$
As $d B / d t$ is constant, therefore $d \phi / d t$ is also constant.
So, the current induced is constant.
Whenever there is a change in flux, the body tries to oppose this change by inducing current in opposite direction.
Hence, the correct option is (2).

options.text: constant and it will be in the direction of $i$.
constant and it will be in a direction opposite to the $i$.
increased with time and it will be in the direction of $i$.
increased with time and opposite to the direction of $i$

subject: Physics

question: In a pair of adjacent coils, if the current in one coil changes from $10 A$ to $2 A$ in a time $0.2 s$, an emf of  $120{\text{ }}V$  is induced in another coil. The mutual inductance of the pair of the coils is
explanation:
Given, Current change, $\Delta i=10-2=8 {~A}$
Time $=0.2 s$
$E M F$ induced $=120 {~V}$
Using Faraday's law of mutual induction,
$|e|=\dfrac{d \phi}{d t}$
As $\phi=M \Delta i$
[ $M$ is mutual inductance of pair]
$e = \frac{{M\Delta i}}{{\Delta T}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Put the value in Eq. (1)
$120=\dfrac{M \times 8}{0.2} \Rightarrow M=\dfrac{120}{40} \Rightarrow M=3 H$
subject: Physics

question: The self inductance of a coil depends on
explanation:
Self inductance of a solenoidal coil is given
as $L=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{l}$
Where, $\mu_{0}$ is permeability of free space,
$N=$ no. of turns
$A=$ Area of cross section of coil
$l=$ length of the coil
Thus, self inductance of a coil depends on size, shape of the coil, and number of turns in it.

options.text: number of turns of the coil only
size, shape of the coil and number of turns in it

subject: Physics

question: A varying current in a coil changes from $10 A$ to zero in  $1.5{\text{ }}s.$  If the average emf induced in the coil is  $200{\text{ }}V,$  the self-inductance of the coil is
explanation:
Self-induced emf in coil is given by
$e=\left|L\left(\dfrac{d i}{d t}\right)\right|$
Here, $E=200 V, \dfrac{d i}{d t}=\left(\dfrac{10-0}{1.5}\right) A / s$
So, self-inductance of coil,
$L=\dfrac{e}{d i / d t}=\dfrac{200}{\left(\dfrac{10-0}{1.5}\right)}$
$\Rightarrow \quad L=\dfrac{200 \times 1.5}{10}=30 H$
Hence, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A circular coil consists 70 closely wound turns and has a radius of  $10{\text{ }}cm.$  An externally produced magnetic field of magnitude $2 \times 10^{-3} T$ is applied perpendicular to the coil. The net flux through the coil is found to vanish when the current in the coil is  $2.2{\text{ }}A.$  The inductance of the coil is
explanation:
Given, number of turns in the circular coil,
$N=70, r=10 {~cm}=10^{-1} {~m}$,
$B=2 \times 10^{-3} T, \theta=0^{\circ}$.
Magnetic flux, $\phi=N B A \cos \theta$
$=70 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times \pi r^{2} \times \cos 0^{\circ}$
$=140 \pi \times 10^{-3} \times\left(10^{-1}\right)^{2}=140 \pi \times 10^{-5}$
$=140 \times \dfrac{22}{7} \times 10^{-5}=4.4 \times 10^{-3} {~Wb}$
We know that,
$\phi=L i$
$\Rightarrow L=\dfrac{\phi}{i}=\dfrac{4.4 \times 10^{-3}}{2.2}=2 \times 10^{-3} {H}=2 {mH}$
Hence, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: Two concentric circular coils, one of small radius $r$ and the other of large radius $R$ are placed co-axially with centres coinciding. If the radius is changed by  $2\,\% ,$  then the change in mutual inductance of the arrangement is
explanation:
Mutual inductance, $M=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \pi N_{1} N_{2} r^{2}}{2 R}$
where, $N_{1}=$ number of turns in inner coil,
$N_{2}=$ number of turns in outer coil,
$r=$ radius of inner coil
and $R=$ radius of outer coil
$M \propto r^{2}$
So, by the formula of error analysis
$\dfrac{\Delta M}{M} \times 100 \%=2\left(\dfrac{\Delta r}{r} \times 100 \%\right)$
Given, $\dfrac{\Delta r}{r}=2 \%$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta M}{M} \times 100 \%=2(2 \%)=4 \%$
Hence, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: Consider a current in a circuit falls from  $6.0{\text{ }}A$  to  $1.0{\text{ }}A$  in  $0.2{\text{ }}s.$  If an average emf of  $150{\text{ }}V$  is induced by the circuit, then the self inductance of the circuit is
explanation:
Given, $i_{1}=6 A, i_{2}=1 A, \Delta t=0.2 s$ and $e=150 {~V}$
Average emf induced in an inductor,
$e=L \dfrac{\left(i_{1}-i_{2}\right)}{\Delta t}$
Self inductance,
$L=\dfrac{e \cdot \Delta t}{i_{1}-i_{2}}=\dfrac{150 \times 0.2}{6-1}=\dfrac{15 \times 2}{5}$
$\Rightarrow \quad L=6 H$
Hence, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: Consider a toroid with rectangular cross section, of inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$ and height $h$, carrying $n$ number of turns. Then the self-inductance of the toroidal coil when current $I$ passing through the toroid is
explanation:
Given, a toroid with a rectangular cross - section of inner radius $a$ and outer radius $b$.
Height of the toroid $=h$
Magnetic field inside a rectangular toroid is given by
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952844/preptracker/questions/q_112274_explanation_1764952844195.png
using the infinitesimal cross-sectional area element,
$d x=h d r$
$\therefore$ Flux passing through the cross-section of toroid
$\phi=\int B \cdot d x=\int_{a}^{b} \dfrac{\mu_{0} n i}{2 \pi r} \cdot(h d r)$
$\phi=\dfrac{\mu_{0} n i h}{2 \pi} \int_{a}^{b} \dfrac{1}{r} d r$
$\Rightarrow \phi=\dfrac{\mu_{0} n h i}{2 \pi}[\log r]_{a}^{b}$
$\Rightarrow \phi=\dfrac{\mu_{0} n h i}{2 \pi}(\ln b-\ln a)$
$\phi=\dfrac{\mu_{0} n h i}{2 \pi} \ln \left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$
Now, self inductance of rectangular toroid,
$L=\dfrac{n \phi}{i}$
Putting the value of $\phi$, we get
$L = \frac{{{\mu _0}{n^2}h}}{{2\pi }}\ln \left( {\frac{b}{a}} \right)$
Hence, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A solenoid of radius $R$ has $n$ turns per unit length, then the self-inductance of the solenoid per unit length is
explanation:
Self-induction of solenoid, $L=\dfrac{N \phi}{i}$
where, $\phi=$ magnetic flux,
$N=$ total numbers of turns and $i=$ current.
Here, $N=n l, n=$ number of turns per unit length and $l=$ length of solenoid.
As, $\phi=\vec{B} \vec{A}=\left(\mu_{0} n i\right) \pi R^{2}, [$ where, $R=$ radius $]$
$L=\dfrac{(n l)\left(\mu_{0} n i\right) \pi R^{2}}{i}$
$\Rightarrow L=\left(\mu_{0} n^{2} \pi R^{2}\right) l$
$\therefore \dfrac{L}{l}=\mu_{0} n^{2} \pi R^{2}$
Hence, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: Self-inductance depends on:
explanation:
$L=\mu_{r} \mu_{0} n^{2} A l$
$\mu_{r}=$ relative permeability (medium)
$A l=$ Volume (geometry)
So. Correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: In a coil, the current changes from $-2 A$ to $2 A$ in $0.2 s$ and induces an emf of $0.1\,V.$ The self inductance of the coil is
explanation:
Given: $dt = 0.2\,s,e = 0.1\,V$
Induced emf in a inductor is $\varepsilon=-L \dfrac{d i}{d t}$
Change in current in a coil, $di = 2 - ( - 2) = 4\,A$
$e=L \dfrac{d i}{d t}$
$ \Rightarrow 0.1 = L \times \frac{4}{{0.2}};L = 5\,mH$
subject: Physics

question: Two conducting circular loops $A$ and $B$ are placed in the same plane with their centres coinciding as shown in figure. The mutual inductance between them is
explanation:
Let current in the outer loop $B$ be $i$.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950755/preptracker/questions/q_44203_explanation_1764950754795.png
Magnetic field at centre of loop $O$ is given as,
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 b}$
Now, magnetic flux through inner coil $A$,
$\phi=\vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}$
$\therefore \phi=B \times \pi a^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \phi=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 b} \times \pi a^{2}$
As per definition of magnetic flux, $\phi=M i$
$\Rightarrow M=\dfrac{\phi}{i}$
$\Rightarrow M=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 b} \times \pi a^{2}$
So, option (1) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: A small square loop of wire of side ${l}$ is placed inside a large square loop of wire of side ${L(>>l)}$. The loops are coplanar and their centres coincide. If the mutual inductance of the system is found to be ${N \sqrt{2} \dfrac{\mu_{0} l^{2}}{\pi L}}$, find the value of ${N}$.
explanation:
Considering the larger loop to be made up of four rods, each of length $L$, the field at the centre, i.e., at a distance $L / 2$ from each rod, will be
$\begin{aligned}& B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi d}[\sin \alpha+\sin \beta] \\& =\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi(L / 2)} 2 \sin 45^{\circ}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi L}\end{aligned}$
Net field: $B_{1}=4 B_{0}=2 \sqrt{2} \dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{\pi L}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950753/preptracker/questions/q_44136_explanation_1764950752857.png So the flux linked with the smaller loop is
$\phi=B_{1} S_{2}=\dfrac{2 \sqrt{2} \mu_{0} I}{\pi L} l^{2}$
Hence, $M=\dfrac{\phi}{I}=2 \sqrt{2} \dfrac{\mu_{0} l^{2}}{\pi L}$
subject: Physics

question: In the circuit as shown, charge ${q}$ varies with time ${t}$ as ${q=\left(t^{2}-4\right)}$ where ${q}$ is in $C$ and time ${t}$ is in seconds. The value of ${V_{A B}}$ at time ${t=3 \sec}$   is
explanation:
${q=t^{2}-4}$
At $t = 3\,\sec ,q$ is positive ${i=\dfrac{d q}{d t}=2 t}$ and ${\dfrac{d i}{d t}=2}$
At $t = 3\sec ,q = 5C$, and ${i=6 {~A}}$
${V_{A}-\dfrac{q}{C}-L \dfrac{d i}{d t}-i R=V_{B}}$
${V_A} - {V_B} = \frac{q}{C} + L\frac{{di}}{{dt}} + iR$
$ = \frac{5}{5} + (0.5)(2) + (6)(1) = 8\,V$
subject: Physics

question: If the inductance of a coil is 1 henry, its effective resistance in a $DC$ circuit will be
explanation:
Given inductance ${L=1 {H}} $
Its inductive reactance ${=X_{L}=\omega L=2 \pi v L}$
For ${D C v=0}$
${\therefore \quad X_{L}=0 \rightarrow}$ effective resistance in ${D C}$ circuit.
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: A solenoid has 2000 turns wound over a length of $0.30\,m$. The area of its cross-section is $1.2 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{m^2}$. Around its central section, a coil of 300 turn is wound. If an initial current of $2\,A$ in the solenoid is reversed in $0.25\,s$, then the emf induced in the coil is
subject: Physics

question: A solenoid $30\,cm$ long is made by winding 2000 loops of wire on an iron rod whose cross-section is $1.5\,c{m^2}$. If the relative permeability of the iron is 600 , what is the self inductance of the solenoid?
subject: Physics

question: Two coils have a mutual inductance $0.005\,H$. The current changes in the first coil according to equation $I=I_{0} \sin \omega t$. where, ${I_0} = 10\,A$ and $\omega  = 100\pi \,rad{s^{ - 1}}.$ The maximum value of emf in the second coil is
explanation:
The emf induced in the second coil is
$\varepsilon=M \dfrac{d I}{d t}$
where $M$ is the mutual inductance between two coils and $\dfrac{d I}{d t}$ is the rate of change of current in the first coil.
As $I=I_{0} \sin \omega t$ (given)
$\therefore \quad \varepsilon=M \dfrac{d}{d t}\left(I_{0} \sin \omega t\right)=M I_{0} \omega \cos \omega t$
The maximum value of emf is
$\varepsilon_{\max }=M I_{0} \omega$
Here, $M = 0.005H,{I_0} = 10\,A,\omega = 100\pi \,rad\,{s^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore {\varepsilon _{\max }} = (0.005H)(10A)\left( {100\pi \,rad{s^{ - 1}}} \right)$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 5\pi V$
subject: Physics

question: The back $emf$ induced in a coil, when current changes from 1 ampere to zero in one milli-second, is $4\,volts$, the self inductance of the coil is
subject: Physics

question: The inductance of a coil is $L = 10H$ and resistance $R = 5\Omega $. If applied voltage of battery is $10\,V$ and it switches OFF in $1\,ms$, then find the value of induced emf of inductor.
explanation:
Amount of magnetic flux linked
with inductor, $\phi = Li$
Now, the emf induced in the inductor
is given by
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,e = - \frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}} = - \frac{d}{{dt}}(Li) = - L\frac{{di}}{{dt}}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,|e| = L\frac{{di}}{{dt}} = L\frac{{\Delta i}}{{\Delta t}}$
Induced current, $\Delta i = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{{10}}{5} = 2\,A$
Circuit switches OFF in $1\,ms$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\Delta t = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}}s$ and $L = 10{\mkern 1mu} H$
$\therefore $ Induced emf in inductor,
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,|e| = 10 \times \frac{2}{{1 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = 2 \times {10^4}\,V$
subject: Physics

question: The current in a self-inductance $L = 40\,mH$ is to be increased uniformly from $1\,A$ to $11\,A$ in $4\,ms$. The induced emf in the inductor during the process is
explanation:
Consider the inductor of inductance $L$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950655/preptracker/questions/q_43839_explanation_1764950654741.png
The current flowing through the inductor is $i$.
Now, we can write,
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\phi = Li$
where, $\phi $ is magnetic flux linked with
the inductor.
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\left| {\frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}}} \right| = L\frac{{di}}{{dt}}$
Given, $L = 40\,mH$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,dt = $ net time difference
${\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = {t_2} - {t_1} = 4\,ms = \Delta t{\mkern 1mu} $
${\mkern 1mu} di\,\,\, = {i_2} - {i_1}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 11 - 1 = 10\,A = \Delta i$
So, ${\mkern 1mu} {\text{|emf|}} = \left| {\frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}}} \right| = \frac{{\Delta \phi }}{{\Delta t}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} $
$\,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} = L\frac{{\Delta i}}{{\Delta t}} = (40mH)\frac{{(10)A}}{{4\,ms}}{\mkern 1mu} $
or induced emf ${\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\, = 10 \times 10 = 100\,V$
subject: Physics

question:
The number of turns, cross-sectional area, and lengths for four solenoids are given in the following table.

The solenoid with maximum self inductance is :
explanation:
${L=\mu_{0} n^{2} \pi r^{2} l}$
$A = \pi {r^2}$
$n = \frac{N}{l} \Rightarrow L = {\mu _0}\frac{{{N^2}}}{l}A$
By putting the given values, it can be seen that it is maximum for solenoid no. 4.
subject: Physics

question: At a given instant, the current and self induced emf in an inductor are directed, as shown in the for an inductor are directed, as shown in the figure. If the induced emf is ${17 V}$ and rate of change of current is ${25 {kA} / {s}}$, choose the correct statement.
explanation: ${e=-L \dfrac{d i}{d t} \Rightarrow L=\dfrac{e}{d i / d t}=680 \mu H}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956498/preptracker/questions/q_43815_explanation_1764956497844.png Also, current is decreasing

options.text: Current is increasing and inductance of coil is ${3.4 \mu H}$
Current is decreasing and inductance of coil is ${680 \mu H}$
Current is decreasing and inductance of coil is ${3.4 \mu H}$
Current is increasing and inductance of will is ${6.8 \mu H}$

subject: Physics

question: A square loop of side length ' ${a}$ ' is moving away from an infinitely long current carrying conductor at a constant speed ' ${v}$ ' as shown. Let ' ${x}$ ' be the instantaneous distance between the long conductor and side ${A B}$. The mutual inductance \[(M)\] of the (square loop - long conductor) pair changes with time ( t ) according to which of the following graphs?
explanation:
By $ = $ produced by the Current carrying straight wire https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957122/preptracker/questions/q_43784_explanation_1764957122291.png $ = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi y}}$
Consider a small rectangular element of width $dy.$
$dA = ady$
$d{\phi _B} = {\text{By}}\,\,dA = \frac{{{\mu _0}Ia}}{{2\pi }}\,\,\frac{{dy}}{y}$
$ \Rightarrow {\phi _B} = \frac{{{\mu _0}Ia}}{{2\pi }}\int_x^{x + a} {\frac{{dy}}{y} = } \frac{{{\mu _0}Ia}}{{2\pi }}\,\,\ln \left( {\frac{{x + a}}{x}} \right).$
$\therefore M = \frac{{{\phi _B}}}{I} = \frac{{{\mu _0}Ia}}{{2\pi }}\,\,\ln \left( {1 + \frac{a}{{vt}}} \right)$
The graph between mutual inductance and time $t$ is
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957123/preptracker/questions/q_43784_explanation_1764957123295.png
Correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: Find the mutual inductance in the arrangement, when a small circular loop of wire of radius ' $R$ ' is placed inside a large square loop of wire of side $L(L>>R)$. The loops are coplanar and their centres coincide
explanation:
Now, field at centre of outer square
$B = \frac{{2\sqrt 2 {\mu _0}I}}{{\pi L}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Where current in the square loop is I.
Flux with small loop $(\phi)=B \cdot \pi R^{2}$
$\phi=\dfrac{2 \sqrt{2} \mu_{0} I}{\pi L} \cdot \pi R^{2}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950625/preptracker/questions/q_43724_explanation_1764950625520.png $\phi = \frac{{2\sqrt 2 {\mu _0}I{R^2}}}{L}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Now, mutual induction, $M=\dfrac{\phi}{I}$
$M=\dfrac{2 \sqrt{2} \mu_{0} I R^{2}}{L I} \Rightarrow M=\dfrac{2 \sqrt{2} \mu_{0} R^{2}}{L}$
subject: Physics

question: A $12\,V$ battery connected to a coil of resistance $6 \Omega$ through a switch, drives a constant current in the circuit. The switch is opened in $1\,ms$. The emf induced across the coil is $20\,V$. The inductance of the coil is
explanation:
Given, $V = 12V,R = 6\Omega ,t = 1\,ms,\varepsilon = 20\,V$
As the steady current, $i = \frac{{12}}{6} = 2\,A$
The final current is zero.
So, $\frac{{di}}{{dt}} = \frac{{0 - 2}}{{1 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = - 2 \times {10^3}\,A/s$
By Faraday's Law
$\varepsilon = - \frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}} = - \frac{{d(il)}}{{dt}} = - L\frac{{di}}{{dt}}$
$ \Rightarrow 20 = L \times 2 \times {10^3}\,\,;$
$L = {10^{ - 2}}H = 10mH$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: When number of turns in a solenoid is doubled, coefficient of self inductance of the solenoid coil becomes four times.

Reason: Coefficient of self inductance does not depend on number of turns of solenoid.
explanation: We know $L=\mu_{o} \mu_{r} N^{2} A \Rightarrow L$ depends on $\mathrm{N}$. $\Rightarrow$ When the number of turns in a coil $(N)$ is doubled, the coefficient of self-inductance $(L)$ becomes four times larger. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: An electric lamp is connected in series with a long solenoid of copper with aircore and then connected to $A C$ source. If an iron rod is inserted in solenoid the lamp will become dim.

Reason: If an iron rod is inserted in solenoid, the inductance of solenoid increases.
explanation:
With air - core $L_{0}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{l}$
With iron - core $L=\mu_{r} L_{0}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956461/preptracker/questions/q_43602_explanation_1764956461246.png For iron: $\mu_{r}=5500>>1$. When an iron rod is inserted into a solenoid, the inductance of the solenoid increases. As a result, the reason is also valid, stating that the lamp connected in series with the solenoid becomes dim when connected to an $A C$ source. So correct option is $(1)$.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Self induction arises when strength of current changes in same coil.

Reason: In mutual induction, current changes in one coil effects the current in other coil also.
explanation: Self-induction is an inherent property of each coil . Assertion is correct. In mutual induction a change of current in one coil produces current change in other coil. So correct option is $(2)$.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: When two coils are wound on each other the mutual induction between the coils is maximum.

Reason: Mutual induction does not depend on the orientation of the coils.
explanation: The assertion is accurate when it describes that when two coils are wound closely on each other, maximum flux linkage occurs and so it maximizes mutual induction between them. But, the reason is false. The orientation of the coils does impact mutual induction to some extent. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Self-inductance is called the inertia of electricity.

Reason: Self-inductance is the phenomenon, according to which an opposing induced e.m.f. is produced in a coil as a result of change in current or magnetic flux linked in the coil.
explanation:
As inertia in mechanics a change in state of rest or motion self-inductance $(L)$ resists changes in electrical current within a circuit or coil. The reason is also correct, as it explains that self-inductance involves the production of an opposing electromotive force $(e m f)$ within a coil when there are changes in current $(I)$ or magnetic flux $(\phi) . V_{\text {induced }}=-\dfrac{d \phi}{d t}=-L \dfrac{d I}{d t}$.
The negative sign is for Assertion to be true. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Inductance coil can be made of copper.

Reason: Ohm's law can be used to find induced current in the coil.
explanation: Copper has good electrical conductivity and suitability for carrying electrical currents with low resistance, which helps reduce energy losses. The reason is also valid as it explains that wires with lower resistance allow for more significant induced current when for same induced voltage than wires of larger resistance $\left(I_{\text {ind }}=\dfrac{V_{\text {ind }}}{R_{\text {coil }}}\right)$. So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: Two coils $A$ and $B$ are placed in a circuit. When current in the coil $A$ changes by $0.8\,A$ the magnetic flux in coil $B$ changes by $0.16\,Wb$. The mutual inductance between the coils is
explanation:
As, $\phi=MI$
$M=\dfrac{\phi}{I}=\dfrac{0.16}{0.8}=0.2 H$
subject: Physics

question: The current in a coil changes from $2 A$ to $5 A$ in$0.3\,s.$ The magnitude of emf induced in the coil is $1.0\,V.$ The value of self-inductance of the coil is
explanation:
$\Delta I=3 A \quad t=0.3$
$V_{\text {induced }}=-L \dfrac{d I}{d t}$
$\Rightarrow 1=L \times \dfrac{3}{0.3}$
$ \Rightarrow L = \frac{1}{{10}} = 0.1\,H = 100\,mH$
Correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: When current in a coil changes from $5\,\,A$ to $2\,\,A$ in $0.1\,\,s$, an average voltage of $50\,\,V$ is produced. The self inductance of the coil is :
explanation:
$$I_{1}=5 A \& I_{2}=2 A$$
$$\begin{aligned}& \Delta I=2-5=-3 A \\& \Delta t=0.1 \mathrm{sec}\end{aligned}$$
$$\varepsilon=-L \dfrac{d i}{d t}$$
$$50=-L\left(\dfrac{-3}{0.1}\right) \Rightarrow L=1.67 H$$
subject: Physics

question: Find the mutual inductance between two rectangular loops, shown in the figure
explanation:
Let $i$ be the current flowing in the large rectangle. Consider a small rectangle of width $dx$ as shown in the figure.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950555/preptracker/questions/q_43327_explanation_1764950554853.png
The flux through the strip is
$$\begin{aligned}& d \phi=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i}{2 \pi}\left[\dfrac{1}{x}-\dfrac{1}{x+a}\right] b d x \\& \phi=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i b}{2 \pi} \int_{c}^{c+b}\left[\dfrac{1}{x} d x-\dfrac{1}{x+a} d x\right] \\& \phi=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i b}{2 \pi}\left[\ln \left(\dfrac{c+b}{c}\right)-\ln \left(\dfrac{a+b+c}{a+c}\right)\right]=M i \\& M=\dfrac{\mu_{o} b}{2 \pi}\left[\ln \left(\dfrac{c+b}{c}\right)-\ln \left(\dfrac{a+b+c}{a+c}\right)\right]\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: Two coils $P$ and $Q$ are kept near each other. When no current flows through coil $P$ and current increases in coil $Q$ at the rate $10\,\,A/s$, the emf in coil $P\,{\text{is}}\,15\,mV$. When coil $Q$ carries no current and current of $1.8\,\,A$ flows through coil $P$, the magnetic flux linked with the coil $Q$ is
explanation:
Given, $\frac{{d{I_Q}}}{{dt}} = 10\,A/s$
${e_p} = 15\,mA$
$ = 15 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,V$
Induced emf in coil $P$ is given as$\left|e_{p}\right|=M \cdot \dfrac{d l_{Q}}{d t}$
Substituting the given values, we get
$15 \times 10^{-3}=M \times 10$
$\Rightarrow M=15 \times 10^{-4} H$
when ${I_p} = 1.8\,A$, magnetic flux linked with coil $Q$ is given as,
${\phi _Q} = M{l_p} = 15 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times 1.8$
$ = 27.0 \times {10^{ - 4}} = 2.7mWb$
subject: Physics

question: A pair of adjacent coils has a mutual inductance of  $2.5\,H$. If the current in one coil changes from 0 to $40\,A$ in $0.8\,s$, then the change in flux linked with the other coil is
explanation:
Given, $M = 2.5H, = \frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = \frac{{40 - 0}}{{0.8}} = 50A{s^{ - 1}}$
Also, $$\varepsilon=M \dfrac{d I}{d t}=\dfrac{d \phi}{d t}$$
or $d\phi = MdI = 2.5(40 - 0) = 100\,\,Wb$
subject: Physics

question: Two coils have a mutual inductance of $0.01\,H$. The current in the first coil changes according to equation $I=5 \sin 200 \pi t$. The maximum value of emf induced in the second coils is
explanation:
Given, mutual inductance in the coil, $M = 0.01H$ and current in the first coil, $I=5 \sin 200 \pi t$ Emf induced in secondary coil is given
$e=M \dfrac{d I}{d t}$
Putting the values in above relation,
$\begin{aligned}& e=0.01 \times \dfrac{d}{d t}(5 \sin 200 \pi t) \\& =0.01 \times 5 \times 200 \pi \cos 200 \pi t=10 \pi \cos 200 \pi t\end{aligned}$
Hence, from the equation of emf, the maximum value of emf induced in the coil is ${e_0} = 10\pi V$
subject: Physics

question: The coefficient of mutual inductance of two coils is $6\,mH$. If the current flowing in one is 2 ampere, then the induced e.m.f. in the second coil will be:
explanation: Secondary e.m.f. induces only when current through primary changes.
subject: Physics

question: The physical quantity which is measured in the unit of $Wb{A^{ - 1}}$ is
explanation:
Units of mutual inductance and self inductance are same, i.e., $H$ or $Wb/A$.
$$[M]=[L]=\dfrac{[\phi]}{[I]}$$
subject: Physics

question: The coefficient of self-inductance and the coefficient of mutual inductance have
subject: Physics

question: An air cored solenoid with length $20\,\,cm$, area of cross section $20\,\,c{m^2}$ and number of turns 400 carries a current $2\,\,A.$ The current is suddenly switched off within ${10^{ - 3}}\,s$. The average back emf induced across the ends of the open switch in the circuit is (ignore the variation in magnetic field near the ends of the solenoid)
subject: Physics

question: The current \[(I)\] in the inductance is varying with time according to the plot shown in figure.
explanation:
$$\varepsilon=-L \dfrac{d i}{d t}$$
From 0 to $$\dfrac{T}{2} \dfrac{d i}{d t}=(+) v e$$
$$\Rightarrow \varepsilon=(-)$$ ve From $$\dfrac{T}{2}$$ to $$\mathrm{T}$$
$$\dfrac{d i}{d t}=(-) v e$$
$$\Rightarrow \varepsilon=(+) v e$$
subject: Physics

question: The current $$i$$ in an inductor varies with time as shown in the figure. The variation of induced emf with time would be
explanation:
$$\varepsilon=-\mathrm{L} \dfrac{\mathrm{di}}{\mathrm{dt}}$$
During 0 to $$\dfrac{\mathrm{T}}{4}, \dfrac{\mathrm{di}}{\mathrm{dt}}=(+) \mathrm{ve}$$ So, $$\varepsilon$$ is -ve
For, $$\dfrac{\mathrm{T}}{4}$$ to $$\dfrac{\mathrm{T}}{2}, \dfrac{\mathrm{di}}{\mathrm{dt}}=0 \Rightarrow \varepsilon=0$$
$$\dfrac{\mathrm{T}}{2}$$ to $$\dfrac{3 \mathrm{~T}}{4} \quad \dfrac{\mathrm{di}}{\mathrm{dt}}=(-) \mathrm{ve}$$
So $$\varepsilon$$ is $$(+)$$ ve
subject: Physics

question: The length of a wire required to manufacture a solenoid of length $$l$$ and self-induction $L$ is (crosssectional area is negligible)
explanation:
$$L=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{l}$$
If $$x$$ is the length of the wire to manufacture the solenoid with $$\mathrm{r}$$ as radius,
then $$\quad x=2 \pi r N, A=\pi r^{2}$$
$$\therefore L=\mu_{0}\left(\dfrac{x^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} r^{2}}\right) \dfrac{\pi r^{2}}{l} \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{\dfrac{4 \pi L l}{\mu_{0}}}$$
subject: Physics

question: A coil having an inductance of $0.5\,H$ carries a current which is uniformly varying from zero to 10 ampere in 2 second. The e.m.f. (in volts) generated in the coil is:
explanation: $|e| = L\left| {\frac{{di}}{{dt}}} \right| = 0.5 \times \frac{{10}}{2} = 2.5\,V$
subject: Physics

question: Current in a coil changes from $4\,A$ to zero in 0.1 second and the emf induced is $100\,\,V$. The self inductance of the coil is
explanation:
Induced e.m.f $$=-($$ Inductance $$\times$$ Rate of change of current)
$\therefore \varepsilon = - L\frac{{dI}}{{dt}}{\text{ }}$
$\,{\text{or}}\,100 = \frac{{ - L(0 - 4)}}{{0.1}}{\text{ }}\,{\text{or}}\,L = \frac{{100 \times 0.1}}{4} = \frac{5}{2}$
$ = 2.5H.$
subject: Physics

question: When the number of turns and the length of the solenoid are doubled keeping the area of crosssection same, the inductance
explanation:
$$L=\mu_{0} \dfrac{N^{2}}{l} A$$. When $$N$$ and $$l$$ are doubled.
$$L$$ is also doubled.
subject: Physics

question: Two coils $A$ and $B$ have mutual inductance $2 \times 10^{-2}$ Henry. If the current in the primary is $i = 5\sin 10\pi t$, then the maximum value of emf induced in coil $B$ is
explanation:
$M=2 \times 10^{-2} H \text { and } i=5 \sin 10 \pi t$
Induced emf, $e_{B}=-M \dfrac{d i_{A}}{d t}$
Where, $e_{B}=$ emf in coil $B$
$\begin{aligned}& e_{B}=-2 \times 10^{-2} \dfrac{d}{d t}[5 \sin 10 \pi t] \\& {[i=5 \sin 10 \pi t]} \\& e_{B}=-2 \times 5 \times 10^{-2} \times 10 \pi[\cos 10 \pi t] \\& e_{B}=-\pi[\cos 10 \pi t]\end{aligned}$
The maximum value of emf induced in coil
$\mathrm{B}=\pi \text { volt }$
subject: Physics

question: Two coils have a mutual inductance $0.005\,H.$ the current changes in the first coil according to the equation $i = {i_m}\sin \omega t$ where ${i_m} = 10\,A$ and $\omega  = 100\,\pi rad{s^{ - 1}}.$ The maximum value of the emf induced (V) in the second coil is]
explanation:
The emf induced in the second coil is
$\varepsilon = - M\frac{{di}}{{dt}}$
where $$M$$ is the mutual inductance of two coils, $\frac{{di}}{{dt}}$ is the rate of change of current in the first coil.
But $$\dfrac{d i}{d t}=\dfrac{d}{d t}\left(i_{m} \sin \omega t\right)=i_{m} \omega \cos \omega t$$
$$|\varepsilon|=\left|M i_{m} \omega \cos \omega t\right|$$
For maximum value of emf induced,
$$|\cos \omega t|=1$$
The maximum value of the emf induced is
$$\varepsilon_{\max }=\mathrm{Mi}_{\mathrm{m}} \omega$$
Here,
$M = 0.005H,{i_m} = 10\,A,\omega = 100\pi \,rad{s^{ - 1}}$
${\varepsilon _{{\rm{max}}}} = (0.005H)(10\,A)(100\pi ) = 5\pi V$
subject: Physics

question: The current following through an inductance coil of self inductance $6\,mH$ at different time instants is as shown. The emf induced between $t = 20\,s$ and $t = 40\,s$ is nearly
explanation:
EMF $$=-($$ Inductance $$\times$$ Rate of change of current)
$\varepsilon = - L\frac{{dI}}{{dt}} = - 6 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times \frac{{3 - 4}}{{40 - 20}}$
$ = 3 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,V$
subject: Physics

question: The length of solenoid is $l$ whose windings are made of material of density $D$ and resistivity $\rho$. The winding resistance is $R$. The inductance of solenoid is $\left[ {m = } \right.$ mass of winding wire, $\mu_{0}=$ permeability of free space]
explanation:
The self inductance of solenoid
$L = {\mu _0}{N^2}\frac{A}{l}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Where, $A$ is the area of cross - section of solenoid and $N$ is number of turns.
If $x$ is the length of wire, then
$R = \frac{{\rho x}}{A}{\text{ }}$
${\text{and }}m = AxD$
$\therefore Rm = \frac{{\rho x}}{A}(AxD)$
$ \Rightarrow x = \sqrt {\frac{{Rm}}{{\rho D}}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
If $r$ is the radius of cross-section of the Solenoid,
$x=2 \pi r N \Rightarrow N=\dfrac{x}{2 \pi r}=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi r} \sqrt{\dfrac{R m}{\rho D}}$
[from eq. (2)]
Substituting values in eq. (1), we get
$\begin{aligned}& L=\mu_{0}\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{R m}}{2 \pi r \sqrt{\rho D}}\right)^{2} \times \dfrac{A}{l}=\mu_{0} \dfrac{R m}{4 \pi^{2} r^{2} \rho D} \cdot \dfrac{\pi r^{2}}{l} \\& =\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi l}\left(\dfrac{R m}{\rho D}\right)\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: What is the self-inductance of solenoid of length $31.4\,\,cm$, area of cross-section ${10^{ - 3}}\,\,{m^2}$ and total number of turns $$10^{3}$$ ?
explanation:
Given, $A = {10^{ - 3}}\,{m^2}$
$l = 31.4\,cm = 31.4 \times {10^{ - 2}}m\,{\rm{and}}\,N = {10^3}$
Magnetic flux,
$$\begin{gathered}\phi=L i \Rightarrow N B A=L i \Rightarrow \dfrac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{\ell}=L i \\L=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{\ell} \Rightarrow L=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times\left(10^{3}\right)^{2} \times 10^{-3}}{31.4 \times 10^{-2}}\end{gathered}$$
$ = 4\,mH$
subject: Physics

question: The current in a coil of inductance $0.2\,H$ changes from $5\,A$ to $2\,\,A$ in $0.5\,\sec $. The magnitude of the average induced emf in the coil is
explanation:
${\text{Given}}\,\,\left| {\frac{{\Delta I}}{{\Delta t}}} \right| = \frac{{5 - 2}}{{0.5}} = \frac{3}{{0.5}} = 6A{s^{ - 1}}$
and $L = 0.2H$
$$\therefore$$ Induced emf, $|\varepsilon | = L\left| {\frac{{\Delta l}}{{\Delta t}}} \right| = 0.2 \times 6 = 1.2\,V$
subject: Physics

question: Two conducting circular loops of radii $$R_{1}$$ and ${R_2}$ are placed in the same plane with their centres coinciding. If ${R_1} >  > {R_2}$, the mutual inductance $M$ between them will be directly proportional to
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950497/preptracker/questions/q_43024_explanation_1764950497165.png
Flux through the smaller loop is $$\phi=M I$$
$$\begin{gathered}B \pi R_{1}^{2}=M I \\\left(\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R_{2}}\right) \pi R_{1}^{2}=M I \Rightarrow M=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \pi R_{1}^{2}}{2 R_{2}}\end{gathered}$$
subject: Physics

question: A long solenoid has 1000 turns. When a current of  $4\,A$ flows through it, the magnetic flux linked with each turn of the solenoid is $4 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,\,Wb$. The self inductance of the solenoid is
explanation:
$$\therefore$$ Total flux linked $ = 1000\left[ {4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right]\,Wb$
$$\phi_{t o t a l}=4 \Rightarrow L i=4 \Rightarrow L=1 H$$
subject: Physics

The self-inductance of a long solenoid is inversely proportional to the area of cross-section.

explanation:

$$N$$ - Total number of turns in the coil.
$$l$$ - Length of the coil
So option (2) is correct

Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

Mutual inductance of a pair of coils depend on their separation as well as their relative orientation.

When the current in a coil changes, it induces a back emf in the same coil.
Back Emf is a measure of the inertia of the coil against the change of current through it.

question:
Find the incorrect statement
I. Inductance is a scalar quantity.
II. Inductance has the dimensions $$\left[M L^{2} T^{2} A^{2}\right]$$
III. The SI unit of inductance is henry and it is denoted by $H$.
IV. Inductance is named in honour of Joseph Henry
explanation: I, III and IV are correct. Inductance has dimension $$\left[M L^{2} T^{-2} A^{-2}\right]$$. So option(2) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: A pair of adjacent coils has a mutual inductance of  $1.5\,H$. If the current in one coil changes from 0 to $20\,\,A$ in $0.5\,s$, what is the change of flux linkage with the other coil?
explanation:
Given, mutual inductance $M = 1.5\,H$
Current change in coil $$d I=20-0=20 A$$
Change in time $dt = 0.5\,\,s$
Induced emf $$\varepsilon=M \dfrac{d I}{d t}=\dfrac{d \phi}{d t}$$
$ \Rightarrow d\phi = MdI = 1.5 \times 20 = 30\,Wb$
Thus, the change of flux linkage is $30\,\,Wb$
subject: Physics

question: The self-inductance $L$ of solenoid of length $$l$$ and area of cross - section $A$, with a fixed number of turns $N$ increases as
explanation:
The self - inductance of a long solenoid of cross-sectional area $A$ and length $$l$$, having $N$ turns is given by
$$L=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{l}$$
subject: Physics

question: The current in an induction coil varies with time $$t$$, according to the graph shown in figure. Which of the following graphs between induced emf $$(\varepsilon)$$ and time is correct?
explanation:
$$\varepsilon=-L \dfrac{d i}{d t}$$
$$\dfrac{d i}{d t}$$ value is $$0,(-)$$ ve and $$(+)$$ ve and hence $$\varepsilon$$ value is $$0,(+)$$ ve and (-)ve
subject: Physics

question: \[A\] and \[B\] are two coils placed closely as shown. The current in coil A varies as shown in the graph.
explanation:
The induced emf in the coil $$B$$ is
$$\varepsilon_{B}=-M \dfrac{d i_{A}}{d t}$$
$$i_{A}$$ - current flow through the coil $A$
$$i_{A}=$$ constant $$\Rightarrow \varepsilon_{B}=0$$
$$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{d i_{A}}{d t}=(+) v e \Rightarrow \varepsilon_{B}=(-) v e \\& \dfrac{d i_{A}}{d t}=(-) v e \Rightarrow \varepsilon_{B}=(+) v e\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: Two coaxial solenoids are made by winding a thin insulated wire over a pipe of cross sectional area $A = 10\,\,c{m^2}$ and length $20\,\,cm$. If one of the solenoids has 300 turns and the other 400 turns, their mutual inductance is $\left( {{\mu _0} = 4\pi  \times {{10}^{ - 7}}\,Tm/A} \right)$
explanation:
Let $$N_{1}$$ and $$N_{2}$$ are the total number of turns in the two solenoids. Let $A$ is the area of cross section of the inner solenoid. The flux through the inner solenoid is
${\phi _2} = M{i_1} = {N_2}{B_1}A = {N_2}\left( {\frac{{{\mu _0}{i_1}{N_1}}}{l}} \right)A = M{i_1}$
$M = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}{N_2}}}{l} = 2.4\pi \times {10^{ - 4}}H$
subject: Physics

question: Find the mutual inductance of a straight long wire and a rectangular loop, as shown in the figure
explanation:
Consider a rectangular strip of width $dx$ and length $b$. Let $$i$$ be the current through the straight wire
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957121/preptracker/questions/q_42836_explanation_1764957120701.png
Flux through the strip is
$$\begin{gathered}d \phi=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i}{2 \pi x}(b d x) \\\phi=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i b}{2 \pi x} \int_{x_{o}}^{x_{o}+a} \dfrac{1}{x} d x=\dfrac{\mu_{o} i b}{2 \pi} \ln \left(\dfrac{x_{o}+a}{x_{o}}\right)=M i \\M=\dfrac{\mu_{o} b}{2 \pi} \ln \left(\dfrac{x_{o}+a}{x_{o}}\right)\end{gathered}$$
subject: Physics

question: A solenoid is placed inside another solenoid, the length of both being equal carrying same magnitude of current. The parameters like radius and number of turns are in the ratio 1:2 for the two solenoids. The mutual inductance on each other would be
subject: Physics

question: A circuit contains two inductors of selfinductance $$L_{1}$$ and $$L_{2}$$ in series. If $M$ is the mutual inductance then the effective inductance of the circuit shown will be
explanation:
As the two inductors are in series and flux through them support each other.
$$L=L_{1}+L_{2}+2 M$$
subject: Physics

question: What is the mutual inductance of a two-loop system as shown with centre separation $$l$$ ?
explanation:
Magnetic field at the location of coil (2) produced due to coil (1)
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957119/preptracker/questions/q_42777_explanation_1764957119181.png
$$B=\dfrac{\mu_{o}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2 M}{l^{3}}$$
Flux linked with coil(2)
$$\phi_{2}=B_{1} A_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{o}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{2 i\left(\pi a^{2}\right)}{l^{3}} \times\left(\pi a^{2}\right)$$
Also $$\quad \phi_{2}=M i \Rightarrow M=\dfrac{\mu_{o} \pi a^{4}}{2 l^{3}}$$
subject: Physics

question: If the current $30\,A$ flowing in the primary coil is made zero in $0.1\sec .$ The emf induced in the secondary coil is 1.5 volt. The mutual inductance between the coils is:
explanation: $$e=M \dfrac{d i}{d t} \Rightarrow 1.5=M \times \dfrac{30}{0.1} \Rightarrow M=0.05 H$$
subject: Physics

question: Two coils $A$ and $B$ having turns 300 and 600 respectively are placed near each other, on passing a current of 3.0 ampere in $A$, the flux linked with $A\,{\text{is}}\,1.2 \times {10^{ - 4}}$ weber and with $B\,{\text{it}}\,{\text{is}}\,9.0 \times {10^{ - 5}}$ weber. The mutual inductance of the system is:
explanation:
$$\phi_{2}=M i_{1} \Rightarrow 9 \times 10^{-5}=M \times 3$$
$$\Rightarrow M=3 \times 10^{-5} H$$
subject: Physics

explanation: Mutual inductance of the pair of coils depends on distance between two coils and geometry of two coils.

Relative position and orientation of the two coils
The rates at which the current are changing in two coils
The currents in the two coils

subject: Physics

question: Two solenoids of equal number of turns have their lengths and the radii in the same ratio 1:2. The ratio of their self-inductances will be:
explanation:
Self-inductance,
$$\begin{gathered}L=\dfrac{\mu_{0} N^{2} \pi r^{2}}{l} \Rightarrow \dfrac{L_{1}}{L_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\right)^{2}\left(\dfrac{l_{2}}{l_{1}}\right)=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{2} \times\left(\dfrac{2}{1}\right) \\\dfrac{L_{1}}{L_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2}=1: 2\end{gathered}$$
subject: Physics

question: A coil of wire of a certain radius has 600 turns per unit length and a self-inductance of $108\,mH.$ The self-inductance of a $$2^{n d}$$ similar coil of 500 turns per unit length will be
explanation: $\frac{{{L_B}}}{{{L_A}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{n_B}}}{{{n_A}}}} \right)^2} \Rightarrow {L_B} = {\left( {\frac{{500}}{{600}}} \right)^2} \times 108 = 75mH$
subject: Physics

question: A solenoid of length $$l$$ metre has self-inductance $$L$$ henry. If number of turns are doubled, its self-inductance
subject: Physics

question: A solenoid of length $$l$$ with $$n$$ turns per unit length has self inductance $L$. If both $$n$$ and $$l$$ are doubled the self inductance will be
subject: Physics

question: A coil of $N = 100$ turns carries a current $I = 5\,A$ and creates a magnetic flux  $\phi  = {10^{ - 5}}T{m^{ - 2}}$ per turn. The value of its inductance $L$ will be:
explanation:
$$N \phi=L i$$
$ \Rightarrow 100 \times {10^{ - 5}} = L \times 5 \Rightarrow L = 0.2mH$
subject: Physics

question: The back e.m.f. induced in a coil, when current changes from 1 ampere to zero in one millisecond, is 4 volts, the self-inductance of the coil is
explanation:
The back emf $$\varepsilon=-L \dfrac{d i}{d t}$$ but $$\varepsilon=4 V$$ and
$\frac{{di}}{{dt}} = \frac{{(0 - 1)}}{{{{10}^{ - 3}}}}$
$\therefore \frac{{ - 1}}{{{{10}^{ - 3}}}}( - L) = 4 \Rightarrow L = 4 \times {10^{ - 3}}henry$
subject: Physics

question: An average induced e.m.f. of $1\,V$ appears in a coil when the current in it is changed from $10\,A$ in one direction to $10A$ in opposite direction in $0.5\,\sec $. Self-inductance of the coil is
explanation: $\left| {{\varepsilon _{\arg {\text{ }}}}} \right| = L\frac{{\Delta i}}{{\Delta t}} \Rightarrow 1 = \frac{{L \times [10 - ( - 10)]}}{{0.5}} \Rightarrow L = 25\,mH$
subject: Physics

question: A coil of $$N=50$$ turns carries a current $I = 1\,A$ and creates a magnetic flux $\phi  = {10^{ - 5}}T{m^{ - 2}}$. The value of its inductance $$L$$ will be
explanation: $\phi  = Li \Rightarrow L = \frac{\phi }{i} = {10^{ - 5}}\,H$
subject: Physics

question: The self inductance of a long solenoid cannot be increased by
explanation: Self-inductance of a long solenoid does not depend upon the current flowing through it.

options.text: Increasing the number of turns in it
Increasing its area of cross section
Changing the medium with greater permeability

subject: Physics

question: When the current changed from $ + 2\,A$ to $ - 2\,A$ in $0.5\,s$, an emf of $8\,V$ is induced in a coil. The coefficient of self-induction of the coil is:
explanation:
Induced emf, $$e=-L \dfrac{d i}{d t}=-L \dfrac{(-2-2)}{0.05}$$
$$8=L \dfrac{(4)}{0.5} \Rightarrow L=\dfrac{8 \times 0.5}{4}=0.1 H$$
subject: Physics

question: A small square loop of wire of side $$l$$ is placed inside a large square loops of wire of side $L(L > l)$. The loops are coplanar and their centres coincide. The mutual inductance of the system is proportional to:
explanation:
Magnetic field produced due to large loop at its centre is
$$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{8 \sqrt{2 i}}{L}$$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950405/preptracker/questions/q_42508_explanation_1764950404779.png
Flux linked with smaller loop
$$\begin{aligned}& \phi=B(l)^{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{8 \pi i l^{2}}{L} \\& \phi=M i \Rightarrow M=\dfrac{\phi}{i}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{8 \sqrt{2} l^{2}}{L} \\& \Rightarrow M \alpha \dfrac{l^{2}}{L}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: Two coils have mutual inductance $0.001H$. The current changes in the first coil according to equation $$I=I_{0} \cos \omega t$$, where ${I_0} = 20\,A$ and $\omega  = 100\pi \,rad\,{s^{ - 1}}$. The maximum value of emf in volt in the second coil is:
explanation:
Mutual inductance between two coils $M = 0.001H$
Peak current, ${I_0} = 20\,A$
Angular frquency, $\omega = 100\pi \,rad\,{s^{ - 1}}$
Current, $$I=I_{0} \cos \omega t$$
$$\begin{aligned}& \left.\Rightarrow \dfrac{d I}{d t}\right|_{\max }=\dfrac{d}{d t}\left(I_{0} \cos \omega t\right)=I_{0} \sin \omega t \cdot \omega \\& \left.\Rightarrow \dfrac{d I}{d t}\right|_{\max }=20 \times 1 \times 100 \pi=2000 \pi\end{aligned}$$
Hence maximum induced emf is given by
$$e=M \times \dfrac{d i}{d t}=0.001 \times 2000 \times \pi=2 \pi V$$
subject: Physics

question: Two coils of self-inductance $$L_{1}$$ and $$L_{2}$$ are placed closer to each other so that total flux in one coil is completely linked with other. If $$M$$ is mutual inductance between them, then
explanation:
$$M=-\dfrac{\varepsilon_{1}}{d i_{1} / d t}=-\dfrac{\varepsilon_{2}}{d i_{2} / d t}$$
Also $$\varepsilon_{1}=-L_{1} \dfrac{d i_{1}}{d t}, \varepsilon_{2}=-L_{2} \dfrac{d i_{2}}{d t}$$
$$\begin{aligned}M^{2} & =\dfrac{\varepsilon_{1} \varepsilon_{2}}{\left(\dfrac{d i_{1}}{d t}\right)\left(\dfrac{d i_{2}}{d t}\right)} \\M^{2} & =L_{1} L_{2} \Rightarrow M=\sqrt{L_{1} L_{2}}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: A mutual inductor consists of two coils $$X$$ and $$Y$$ as shown in figure in which one quarter of the magnetic flux produced by $X$ links with $Y$, giving a mutual inductance $M$. What will be the mutual inductance when $Y$ is used as the primary?
explanation: The mutual inductance $$M$$ remains the same whether $X$ or $Y$ is used as the primary.
subject: Physics

question: Two circular coils can be arranged in any of the three situations shown in the figure. Their mutual inductance will be
explanation: The mutual inductance between two coils depends on their degree of flux linkage. Here, the two coils in arrangement (A) are placed with their planes parallel. This will allow maximum flux linkage and hence maximum mutual inductance.
subject: Physics

question: According to phenomenon of mutual inductance
explanation: Mutual inductance depends on geometry of the coils and property of the medium. Mutual inductance is always defined for a pair.

options.text: The mutual inductance is independent of the magnetic property of the material.
The mutual inductance depends geometry of coils
Ratio of magnetic flux produced by the coil 1 at the place of the coil 2 and the current in the coil 2 will be different from that of the ratio defined by interchanging the coils.
The mutual inductance does not depend on geometry of two coils involved.

question: The inductance of a closely-packed coil of 400 turns is $8\,mH$. A current of $5\,mA$ is passed through it. The magnetic flux through each turn of the coil is:

question: What should be the value of self-inductance of an inductor that should be connected to $220\,V$, so that current changes by $0.9\,A\,{\text{ in}}\,\,0.02\,s$
explanation: $$|e|=\dfrac{L d i}{d t}=220=L \times \dfrac{0.9}{0.2} \Rightarrow L=4.88=5 H$$
subject: Physics

question: The current passing through a choke coil of 5 henry is decreasing at a rate of $2\,A/s$. The e.m.f. developing across the coil is
explanation:
Given $$\dfrac{d i}{d t}=2 A /$$ sec., $$L=5 H$$
$\therefore \quad \varepsilon = L\frac{{di}}{{dt}} = 5 \times 2 = 10\,V$
subject: Physics

question: What is the self-inductance of solenoid of length $31.4\,cm$, area of cross-section ${10^{ - 3}}\,{m^2}$ and total number of turns $$10^{3}$$?
subject: Physics

question: An average induced e.m.f of $1\,V$ appears in a coil when the current in it is changed from $10\,A$ in one direction to $10\,A$ in opposite direction in $0.5\sec $. Self- inductance of the coil is:
explanation: $$|e|=L \dfrac{d i}{d t} \Rightarrow 1=\dfrac{L \times\{10-(-10)\}}{0.5} \Rightarrow L=25 m H$$
subject: Physics

question: If in a coil rate of change of area is $5\,{m^2}/s$ millisecond and current becomes  $1\,amp$ from 2 $amp$ in $2 \times {10^{ - 3}}\sec $. If magnitude of field is 1 tesla then self-inductance of the coil is
explanation:
$$N \phi=L i \Rightarrow \dfrac{N d \phi}{d t}=\dfrac{L d i}{d t} \Rightarrow N B \dfrac{d A}{d t}=\dfrac{L d i}{d t}$$
$$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1 \times 1 \times 5}{10^{-3}}=L \times\left(\dfrac{2-1}{2 \times 10^{-3}}\right) \Rightarrow L=10 H$$
subject: Physics

question: A coil of $N = 100$ turns carries a current $I = 5\,A$ and creates a magnetic flux $\phi  = {10^{ - 5}}T{m^{ - 2}}$per turn. The value of its inductance $L$ will be
explanation: $N\phi  = Li \Rightarrow 100 \times {10^{ - 5}} = L \times 5 \Rightarrow L = 0.2mH$
subject: Physics

question: Metal detector works on the principle of
explanation: Metal detector works on the principle of electromagnetic induction. A metal detector produces a time varying magnetic field from the coil inside. When the hidden metal is exposed to varying magnetic field, it experience a change in flux. As a result  EMF  is induced and eddy current is produced. Since, charge in motion create magnetic field, eddy current produces its own magnetic field. This field is detected by metal detector.
subject: Physics

question: In the circuit of figure, the battery emf is $50\,V$, the resistance is $200\,\Omega $, and the capacitance is $1.0\,\mu F$. The switch ${S}$ is closed for a long time interval, and zero potential difference is measured across the capacitor. After the switch is opened, the potential difference across the capacitor reaches a maximum value of $100\,V$ The value of the inductance is
explanation:
When the switch has been closed for a long time, battery, resistor and coil carry constant current ${I_{i}=\dfrac{\varepsilon}{R}}$. When the switch is opened, current in battery and resistor drops to zero, but the coil carries this same current for a moment as oscillations begin in the ${L C}$ loop. We interpret the problem to mean that the voltage amplitude of these oscillations is ${\Delta V}$, in ${\dfrac{1}{2} C(\Delta V)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} L I_{i}^{2}}$.
Then, $L = \frac{{C{{(\Delta V)}^2}}}{{I_i^2}} = \frac{{C{{(\Delta V)}^2}{R^2}}}{{{\varepsilon ^2}}}$
$ = \frac{{\left( {1.0 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right){{(100)}^2}{{(200)}^2}}}{{{{(50)}^2}}}$
$ = 0.16\,H = 160\,mH$
subject: Physics

question: In the figure as shown, a conducting wire ${P Q}$ of length $1\,m$ is moving in a uniform magnetic field ${B=3 {~T}}$ with constant velocity ${v=2 {~m} / {sec}}$ towards right. Given, $R = 2\,\Omega $, ${C=1 {~F}}$ and $L = 2\,H$. The current through resistance, capacitor and inductor at any time ${t}$ are ${I_{1}, I_{2}}$ and ${I_{3}}$ respectively. The value of ${I_3}$ at time $t = 2\,\sec $ is
explanation:
${V=B V L=(3)(2)(1)=6 {~V}}$
${V_{R}=V_{C}=V_{L}=6 {~V}}$ (All are in parallel)
${V_{L}=L \dfrac{d i}{d t} 6=(2) \dfrac{d i}{d t} \Rightarrow \dfrac{d i}{d t}=3 {~A} / {sec}}$
After $2\,sec$, current will be $6\,A$.
subject: Physics

question: A flat circular coil $10\,cm$ radius has 200 turns. The coil is connected to a capacitor of capacity $20\,\mu F$. It is placed in a uniform magnetic field which decreases at a rate of 0.01 tesla per second. The charge   on the capacitor is
explanation:
${Q=e C}$, where ${e=}$ induced $emf$ ${=\dfrac{d \phi}{d t}}$
${Q=\dfrac{d \phi}{d t} \cdot C=\dfrac{d}{d t}(N A B) C \quad(\therefore \phi=N A B)}$
$Q = NA\frac{{dB}}{{dt}}C$
$ = 200 \times \pi \times {\left( {10 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right)^2}(0.01) \times 20 \times {10^{ - 6}}$
${=1.25 \times 10^{-6}}$ coulomb $ = 1.25\,\mu C$
subject: Physics

question: Spherical insulating ball and a spherical metallic ball of same size and mass are dropped from the same height. Choose the correct statement out of the following (Assume negligible air friction)
explanation: According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction motion of metal is opposed by earth magnetic field due to induced eddy current produced by earth's magnetic field. Hence, insulating ball will reach the earth's surface earlier than the metal ball.

options.text: Both will reach the earth's surface simultaneously.
Insulating ball will reach the earth's surface earlier than the metal ball
Metal ball will reach the earth's surface earlier than the insulating ball
Time taken by them to reach the earth's surface will be independent of the properties of their materials

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The coil in the resistance boxes are made by doubling the wire.

Reason: Only thick wires are to be in any resistance box.
explanation:
Resistance boxes contain resistance values in the range $1\,m\,\Omega $ to $1\,M\,\Omega $
We know that $R=\rho \dfrac{l}{A}$
Thick wire means the value of radius $r$ (in $A=\pi r^{2}$) is large. Then $A$ would be much and value of $R$ decreases. However, as such boxes shall contain low $R$ values as well as high $R$ values, we cannot say that 'only thick wires' to be used. But in order to nullify the effect of inductances ( if any) and thus to have "pure resistances only' it is practice to double the wire. Assertion is correct but reason is not So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: In electric circuits, wires carrying currents in opposite directions are often twisted together.

Reason: If the wires are not twisted together, the combination of wires forms a current loop. The magnetic field generated by the loop might affect adjacent circuits or components.
explanation: There is practice of twisting wires carrying currents in opposite directions in electric circuits. to minimize electromagnetic interference. If they are not twisted together, they can form current loops, leading to strong magnetic fields that may affect nearby circuits or components. Twisting the wires reduces the loop area, (and also such) interference. Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion. So, correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The possibility of an electric bulb fusing is higher at the time switching ON and OFF.

Reason: Inductive effects produces a surge at the time of switch OFF and switch ON
explanation: When switch is OFF and $\mathrm{ON}$, if there are inductive loads (such as transformers and motors) are in circuit, there can be 'sudden variations' (called surges) in the circuit. This can cause counter EMF 's and when potentiall difference across bulb is more than the usual $220 \mathrm{~V}(=\mathrm{rms}$ of $A C$ supply) then bulb will 'Fuse' $\Rightarrow$ Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason explains Assertion.So, correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: A spark occurs between the poles of a switch when the switch is opened.

Reason: Current flowing in the conductor produces magnetic field.
explanation: The assertion correctly describes the occurrence of sparks between the poles of a switch when it is opened as flow of current is suddenly interupted due to a high-voltage arc across the switch contacts, The reason is true. Current flowing through a conductor generates a magnetic field, according to Ampère's circuital law. But 'Reason' is not explanation for 'Assertion'. So correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.

explanation: Coil $A$ carries a steady current with increases in temperature, its resistance increases and so current is decreasing at a constant rate, this induces an emf in $B$ which opposes this change, i.e. current in coil $B$ is in same direction of $A$, therefore they attract to each other.

question: A magnetic field of flux density $1.0\,Wb\,{m^{ - 2}}$ acts normal to a 80 turn coil of $0.01\,{m^2}$ area. If this coil is removed from the field in 0.2 second, the emf induced in it is
explanation:
${\phi _1} = {\rm{BAN}} = 1 \times 0.01 \times 80 = 0.8\,Wb$
${\phi _2} = 0$
$\therefore \varepsilon = - \frac{{\left( {{\phi _2} - {\phi _1}} \right)}}{t} = - \left( {\frac{{0 - 0.8}}{{0.2}}} \right) = 4\,V$

question: Match the concepts related to Lenz's Law and conservation of energy (Column I) with their corresponding effects and outcomes (Column II). Choose the correct option from the given codes.
explanation:
A matches with Q because Lenz's Law states that the induced emf will always be in such a direction that it will oppose the change in magnetic flux that produced it.
B matches with P because when the magnetic flux through the coil increases due to an approaching magnet, Lenz's Law tells us that the induced current will be in such a direction as to create a magnetic field that opposes the increase in flux.
C matches with S because the correct application of Lenz's Law ensures that energy is conserved and that it is not possible to create a perpetual-motion machine, which would violate the law of conservation of energy.
D matches with R because if the North pole of a magnet is moved away from the coil, the decrease in magnetic flux is countered by an induced current flowing in a clockwise direction, which attempts to keep the flux from decreasing. Correct option is (2)

question: Match the statements related to Faraday's experiments and principles (Column I) with their corresponding observations and equations (Column II). Choose the correct answer from the given codes.
explanation:
A matches with Q because Faraday's conclusion from his experiments was that an electromotive force (emf) is induced in a coil when there is a change in magnetic flux through it.
B matches with R because when a magnet moves towards a coil, the change in magnetic flux induces an emf, which causes electric current to flow through the coil.
C matches with S because the mathematical expression given by Faraday's law states that the induced $\operatorname{emf}(\varepsilon)$ in a circuit is equal to the negative rate of change of magnetic flux ( $\phi_{B}$ ) through the circuit.
D matches with P because movement of magnet towards the coil increases the no. of field lines passing through the coil. Correct option is (4)

question: Match the experiments and principles demonstrated by Faraday and Henry (Column I) with their corresponding observations and outcomes (Column II). Choose the correct answer from the given codes.

A - S: Moving a magnet towards or away from a coil induces an electric current whose direction is based on the direction of the magnet's motion, as observed in Faraday's first experiment.
B - R: The relative motion between a coil and a steady magnetic field induces an electric current in the coil, as seen in Faraday's second experiment.
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Q}$ : Changing the current in a stationary coil setup causes a momentary deflection in the galvanometer, indicating that an induced current is present, as demonstrated by Faraday in his third experiment.
D - P: Inserting an iron core into the setup of the experiments increases the deflection in the galvanometer dramatically, showing a stronger induced current due to the iron core's effect on the magnetic field. Correct option is (1)

question: The magnetic field of an electromagnetic   wave obeys the relation in a certain region is $B=10^{-12} \sin \left(5 \times 10^{6} t\right) T$, where $t$ is the time. Then, the induced emf, in a in coil of 300 turns having area $20 {~cm}^{2}$ oriented perpendicular to the field is
explanation:
The emf induced is
$e=-N \dfrac{d \phi}{d t}=-N \dfrac{d(B A)}{d t}=-A N \dfrac{d B}{d t}$
$\Rightarrow e=-20 \times 10^{-4} \times 300 \times \dfrac{d}{d t}\left(10^{-12} \sin 5 \times 10^{6} t\right)$
$ \Rightarrow e = 6000 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times {10^{ - 12}} \times $ $\left( {\cos 5 \times {{10}^6}t} \right) \times 5 \times {10^6}$
$\Rightarrow e=-3 \times 10^{-6} \cos \left(5 \times 10^{6} t\right) V$
Hence, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry
parent_topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry

question: A flat circular coil has 100 turns of wire of radius  $10{\text{ }}cm.$  A uniform magnetic field exists in a direction perpendicular to the plane of the coil and it grows at a rate of  $0.1{\text{ }}T/s.$  The induced emf in the coil is
explanation:
Given: $N=100, r=10 {~cm}=0.1 {~m}$
Rate of change of magnetic field,
$\dfrac{d B}{d t}=0.1 T / s$
$\therefore$ Induced emf in the coil,
$|e|=N \dfrac{d \phi}{d t}=N \cdot \dfrac{d}{d t} B A \quad[\because \phi=B A]$
$=N \dfrac{d}{d t} B \cdot \pi r^{2}=N \cdot \pi r^{2} \cdot \dfrac{d B}{d t}$
$=100 \times \pi \times(0.1)^{2} \times 0.1=\dfrac{\pi}{10} V$
Hence, the correct option is (3).

question: A circular coil of area $100 {~cm}^{2}$ and 20 turns is kept in magnetic field of flux density $2 {~Wb}-{m}^{2}$. It rotates from a position where its plane makes an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the field to a position perpendicular to the field in a time of $0.2 s$. Find the magnitude of the emf induced in the coil due to its rotation.
explanation:
Given that, area of coil, $A = 100\,c{m^2}$
$ = 100 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,{m^2},N = 20$ & $B = 2\,Wb - {m^2}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952835/preptracker/questions/q_112302_explanation_1764952835296.png Now, induced emf, $e=-\dfrac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t}=\dfrac{-\left(\phi_{2}-\phi_{1}\right)}{t}$
$=-\dfrac{\left(N B A \cos 0^{\circ}-N B A \cos 60^{\circ}\right)}{t}$
$=\dfrac{-N B A\left(1-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)}{t}$
$=\dfrac{-B A N}{2 t}=-\dfrac{2 \times 20 \times 100 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 0.2}$
$=-1 {~V}$
So, correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry
parent_topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry

question: A wire loop of area $0.2 m^{2}$ has a resistance of $20 \Omega$. A magnetic field pointing normal to the loop has a magnitude of $0.25 T$ and is reduced to zero at a uniform rate in $10^{-4} {~s}$. What is induced emf and resulting current?
explanation:
Given $A=0.2 m^{2}, R=20 \Omega \&$
$B=0.25 T$
According to Faraday's law of EMI
Induced emf, $e=\dfrac{-d \phi}{d t}$
$=\dfrac{-d}{d t} B A=-A \dfrac{d B}{d t}$
$=-A \dfrac{\Delta B}{\Delta t}=-A \dfrac{\left(B_{2}-B_{1}\right)}{10^{-4}}$
$=-0.2 \dfrac{(0-0.25)}{10^{-4}}=\dfrac{0.05}{10^{-4}} \Rightarrow e=500 {~V}$
and induced current, $I=\dfrac{e}{R}=\dfrac{500}{20}=25 {~A}$

question: A conducting circular coil is placed in a uniform magnetic field with the magnetic field initially directed perpendicular to the plane of the coil. explanation:
Induced emf is given by
$\begin{aligned}& e=\dfrac{-d \phi}{d t}=-\dfrac{d}{d t} \cdot(B A \cos \theta) \\& \Rightarrow e=+B A \sin \theta\end{aligned}$
$\dfrac{e_{1}}{e_{2}}=\dfrac{\sin \theta_{1}}{\sin \theta_{2}}$
Here, $\theta_{1}=\left(90^{\circ}-60^{\circ}\right)=30^{\circ}$
$\theta_{2}=180^{\circ}-120^{\circ}=60^{\circ}$
$\dfrac{e_{1}}{e_{2}}=\dfrac{\sin 30^{\circ}}{\sin 60^{\circ}}=\dfrac{1 / 2}{\sqrt{3} / 2}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
subject: Physics
topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry
parent_topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry

question: A long solenoid has 20 turns per  $cm.$   A small loop of area $\dfrac{4}{\pi} {~cm}^{2}$ is placed inside the solenoid normal to its axis. If the current carried by the solenoid changed steadily from  $1.0{\text{ }}A$  to  $3.0{\text{ }}A$  in $0.2 s$, what is the magnitude of the induced emf in the loop?
explanation:
Given, number of turns per $cm$ in long solenoid, $n=20$ turns $/ {cm}=2000$ turns $/ {m}$
Area of loop, $A=\dfrac{4}{\pi} {~cm}^{2}=\dfrac{4}{\pi} \times 10^{-4} {~m}^{2}$
$\Delta i=3-1=2 A$
$\Delta t=0.2 s$
$\therefore$ Magnetic field inside the solenoid,
$B = {\mu _0}ni\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Magnetic flux linked with loop kept inside the solenoid normal to it,
$\phi=B A$
$=\mu_{0} n i A$ [from Eq. (1)]
$\therefore$ According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, magnitude of induced emf is given as
$|e|=\dfrac{d \phi}{d t}=\dfrac{d}{d t}\left(\mu_{0} n i A\right)=\mu_{0} n A \dfrac{d i}{d t}$
$\Rightarrow \quad|e|=\mu_{0} n A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta i}{\Delta t}$
$=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2000 \times \dfrac{4}{\pi} \times 10^{-4} \times \dfrac{2}{0.2}$
$=3.2 \times 10^{-6} V=3.2 \mu V\left(\because 10^{-6} V=1 \mu V\right)$
Hence, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry
parent_topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry

question: An infinitely long wire lying along the $Y$-axis, is carrying a current $i$ as shown in the figure. The magnetic flux through a circular loop of radius $R$ in the $x y$-plane is [assume, $\mu_{0}=$ permeability of free space]
explanation:
The magnetic field at a point due to a current carrying straight conductor of infinite length is
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958015/preptracker/questions/q_112282_explanation_1764958014596.png
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \pi R}$
The magnetic flux through circular loop around conductor in $x y$ - plane is
$\phi_{B}=B A=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \pi R} \times \pi R^{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i R}{2}$
Hence, the correct option is (2).

question: A $10 \Omega$ coil of 180 turns and diameter $4{\text{ }}cm$  is placed in a uniform magnetic field so that the magnetic flux is maximum through the coil's cross-sectional area. When the field is suddenly removed, a charge of $360 \mu C$ flows through a $618 \Omega$ galvanometer connected to the coil, find the magnitude of magnetic field.
explanation:
Given, resistance of a coil, $R=10 \Omega$
number of turns in the coil, $N=180$ and
diameter of the coil $=4 {~cm}=4 \times 10^{-2} {~m}$
Magnetic flux associated with the coil is maximum, when $\theta=0^{\circ}$
$\therefore$ Magnetic flux, $\phi=B A \cos 0^{\circ}=B A$
When magnetic field is suddenly removed, then some charge flows through the galvanometer.
Given, resistance of a galvanometer,
$R_{g}=618 \Omega$ and charge flowing through the galvanometer, $q=360 \mu {C}=360 \times 10^{-6} {C}$
As, $\quad q=I \cdot \Delta t$
$\Rightarrow \quad q=\dfrac{e}{R_{e q}} \Delta t=\dfrac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta t}{R_{e q}} \quad\left(\because e=\dfrac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t}\right)$
So, for $N$ turns, $q=\dfrac{N}{R_{e q}} \Delta \phi$
Here, $R_{e q}=R+R_{g}$
Putting the given values, we get
$360 \times 10^{-6}=\dfrac{180}{(618+10)} \cdot(B A-0)$
$2 \times 10^{-6} \times 628=B \cdot \pi \times\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow B=\dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 628}{3.14 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}=1 T \quad\left[\because A=\pi r^{2}\right]$
Hence the magnetic field is $1 T$.
Hence, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry
parent_topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry

question: A rectangular loop of wire is placed in the $X Y$ - plane with its side of length  $3{\text{ }}cm$  parallel to the $X$-axis and the side of length  $4\,cm$  parallel to the $Y$-axis. It is moving in the positive $X$ direction with a speed of $10 {~cm} / {s}. A$ magnetic field exists in the space with its direction parallel to the $Z$-axis. The field decreases by $2 \times 10^{-3} {~T} / {cm}$ along the positive $X$-axis and increases in time by $2 \times 10^{-2} {~T} / {s}$. The induced emf in the wire is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952841/preptracker/questions/q_112271_explanation_1764952841473.png According to Faraday's Law of E. M. I. $\,$ :
Induced emf in wire = Rate of change of flux
$=\dfrac{d}{d t}(B A)=A\left\{\left(\dfrac{d}{d t} B\right)+v \dfrac{d}{d x} B\right\}$
$=12 \times 10^{-4}\left(2 \times 10^{-2}+10 \times 10^{-2} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}\right)$
$=12 \times 10^{-4}\left(2 \times 10^{-2}+2 \times 10^{-4}\right)$
$=24 \times 10^{-4}\left(10^{-2}+10^{-4}\right)$
$=0.000024 {~V} \simeq 0 {~V}$
Hence, the correct option is (3).

question: A square loop of side $2 m$ lies in the $Y-Z$ plane in a region having a magnetic field $\vec{B}=(5 \hat{i}+3 j-4 k) T$. The magnitude of magnetic flux through the square loop is
explanation:
Magnetic flux linked with a surface,
$\phi=\vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}$
$\vec{B}=(5 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}-4 \hat{k}) T$.
Area vector along $X$ axis in $Y Z$ plane, $\vec{A}=4 \hat{i} m^{2}$.
$\phi=\vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}$
$\therefore \phi=(5 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}-4 \hat{k}) \cdot 4 \hat{\imath} W b$
$=20 \mathrm{~Wb} \quad(\hat{\imath} \cdot \hat{\imath}=1)$
So correct option is (1).

question: A conducting circular ring is moving with a constant velocity in a uniform magnetic field as shown. Identify the correct graph between induced emf vs time
explanation:
Let $C$ be the centre of the coil and after time $t$ the distance traversed by the loop is $x(=O M)$. From the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952837/preptracker/questions/q_112264_explanation_1764952836767.png $C O=(R-x) . \quad \therefore O N=y=\sqrt{R^{2}-(R-x)^{2}}$
In the next time interval $d t$, change in magnetic flux associated with the ring is given by, $d \phi=B d A$ ( $d A$ - small change in the area of the loop moves inside)
$d \phi=2(O N) \cdot d x \cdot B$
$\therefore \quad d \phi=2 \sqrt{R^{2}-(R-x)^{2}} \cdot d x \cdot B$
$\therefore \quad \varepsilon=\dfrac{d \phi}{d t}=-2 B \sqrt{R^{2}-(R-x)^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{d x}{d t}$
$\Rightarrow \varepsilon=-2 B v \sqrt{R^{2}-(R-x)^{2}}$
Where $\left[\dfrac{d x}{d t}=v\right]$
Squaring on both sides
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{(x-R)^{2}}{R^{2}}+\dfrac{\varepsilon^{2}}{4 B^{2} v^{2} R^{2}}=1$
The distance travelled by the loop in time $t$ is $x=v t$
$\therefore \varepsilon-t$ graph is an ellipse
From equation (1)
At $t=0, \varepsilon=0$ At $t=\dfrac{R}{v}, \varepsilon=2 B v R$
At $t=\dfrac{2 R}{v}, \varepsilon=0$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952838/preptracker/questions/q_112264_explanation_1764952837412.png So correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry
parent_topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry

question: A coil is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field of  $5000\,T.$  When the field is changed to $3000\,T$ in $2\,s,$ an induced emf of $22\,V$ is produced in the coil. If the diameter of the coil is  $0.02\,m,$  then the number of turns in the coil is
explanation:
As per Faraday's law, induced emf,
$\varepsilon=\dfrac{-d \phi}{d t}$
Flux, $\phi=N B A$
Thus, $\varepsilon=-N A \dfrac{d B}{d t}$
$ \Rightarrow \varepsilon = - NA\frac{{\left( {{B_2} - {B_1}} \right)}}{t}$
$\therefore \quad 22 = \frac{{N \times \pi \times {{(0.01)}^2} \times 2000}}{2}\quad \left( {\pi = \frac{{22}}{7}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \quad N = 70$
subject: Physics

parent_topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry

question: A current ${i=2.5(1+2 t) \times 10^{3} {~A}}$ increases at a steady rate in a long straight wire. A small circular loop of radius ${10^{-3} {~m}}$ has its plane parallel to the wire and its centre is placed at a distance of 1 $m$ from the wire. If the resistance of the loop is ${5 \pi \times 10^{-4} \Omega}$, find the magnitude of the induced current  in the loop.
explanation:
The field due to the wire at the centre of loop is
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi d}=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times I}{2 \pi \times 1}=2 I \times 10^{-7} {~W} / {m}^{2}$
So the flux linked with the loop wire
$\phi = BA = B \times \pi {r^2} = 2I \times {10^{ - 7}} \times \pi \times {\left( {{{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)^2}$
$ = 2\pi I \times {10^{ - 13}}\,W$
The magnitude of emf induced in the loop due to change of current
${|e| = \frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}} = 2\pi \times {{10}^{ - 13}}\frac{{dI}}{{dt}}\,V{\text{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)}$
$\because $${I = 2.5(1 + 2t) \times {{10}^3}}$
$\therefore {\text{ }}\frac{{dI}}{{dt}} = 5.0 \times {10^3}\,A/s\quad \quad (2)$
From (1) and (2), we get
$e=2 \pi \times 10^{-13} \times 5.0 \times 10^{3}=10 \pi \times 10^{-10} {~V}$
And the induced current in the loop
$i=\dfrac{e}{R}=\dfrac{10 \pi \times 10^{-10}}{5 \pi \times 10^{-4}}=2.0 \times 10^{-6} {~A}=2.0 \mu {A}$
Due to increase in current in the wire the flux linked with the loop will increase, so in accordance with Len's law the direction of current induced in the loop will be inverse of that in wire, i.e., anticlockwise.
subject: Physics
topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry
parent_topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry

question: A square wire loop with side ${L=1.0 {~m}}$ sides is perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field, with half the area of the loop in the field as shown in the figure. The resistance of the loop is ${35 \Omega}$ and the loop contains an ideal battery with emf ${\varepsilon=6.0 {~V}}$. If the magnitude of the field varies with time according to ${B=5.0-2.0 t}$, with ${B}$ in tesla and ${t}$ in seconds, what is the magnitude of the (net) current (in $A$) around the loop?
explanation:
Let magnetic field at any time be $B$. Let upward direction of area vector as positive, the magnetic flux associated with the circuit is $\Phi_{B}=\dfrac{B L^{2}}{2}$ and the induced emf is $\varepsilon_{i}=-\dfrac{d \Phi_{B}}{d t}=-\dfrac{L^{2}}{2} \dfrac{d B}{d t}$.
As, $B=5.0-2.0 t$, hence, $\dfrac{d B}{d t}=-2.0 {~T} / {s}$
It means the induced emf $\varepsilon_{i}=-\dfrac{(1.0)^{2}}{2}(-2.0)=1.0 {~V}$
As $\varepsilon_{i}$ is positive, so the induced emf is counterclockwise around the circuit, in the same direction as the emf of the battery.
The total emf is
$\varepsilon_{\text {total }}=\varepsilon+\varepsilon_{i}=6.0 {~V}+1.0 {~V}=7.0 {~V}$
The current is in the sense of the total emf (counterclockwise).
The magnitude of the current in the loop
$i=\dfrac{\varepsilon_{\text {total }}}{R}=\dfrac{7.0}{35.0}=0.2 {~A}$
subject: Physics
topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry
parent_topic: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry

question: A closed coil having 20 turns, area of cross-section ${1 {~cm}^{2}}$ and resistance $2\,ohms$ are connected to a ballistic galvanometer of resistance $30\,ohms$. If the normal of the coil is inclined at ${60^{\circ}}$ to the direction of a magnetic field of intensity ${1.5 {~Wb} / {m}^{2}}$, the coil is quickly pulled out of the field to a region of zero magnetic field, the charge  passed through the galvanometer is
explanation:
The total flux linked with the coil having turns ${N}$ and area ${A}$ is
${\phi _{{\rm{initial }}}} = N(\vec B \cdot \vec A) = NBA\cos \theta $
$ = NBA\cos 60^\circ = \frac{{NBA}}{2}$
When the coil is pulled out, the flux becomes zero, ${\phi_{\text {final }}=0}$
The change in flux is ${\Delta \phi=\dfrac{N B A}{2}-0}$
Hence the charge flowed through the circuit is
$q = \frac{{\Delta \phi }}{R} = \frac{{NBA}}{{2R}} = \frac{{20 \times 1.5 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{2 \times 30}}$
$ = 5.0 \times {10^{ - 5}}C = 50.0\,\mu C$

question: A square wire loop of $10.0 \,cm$ side lies at right angles to a uniform magnetic field of $7\,T$. A $10\,V$ light bulb is in a series with the loop as shown in the figure. The magnetic field is decreasing steadily to zero over a time interval ${\Delta t}$. For what value of ${\Delta t}$, the bulb will shine with full brightness?
explanation:
Induced emf should be equal to $10\,V$
${e=A \dfrac{d B}{d t} \Rightarrow 10=\left(\dfrac{10}{100}\right)^{2} \dfrac{7}{\Delta t} \Rightarrow \Delta t=7 {~ms}}$

question: The magnetic flux through a coil perpendicular to its plane is varying according to the relation $\phi=\left(t^{3}+t^{2}+t-6\right) {Wb}$. If the resistance of the coil is $5 \Omega$, calculate the induced current through the coil at $t = 2\,s$ ?
explanation:
$R = 5\,\Omega $
$\phi = \left( {{t^3} + {t^2} + t - 6} \right)Wb$
$\varepsilon = \frac{{ - d\phi }}{{dt}} = - \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {{t^3} + {t^2} + t - 6} \right)$
$ = - \left( {3{t^2} + 2t + 1} \right)V$
${\left. \varepsilon \right|_{t = 2\,s}} = \left[ {3{{(2)}^2} + 2(2) + 1} \right] = - 17\,V$
$i = \frac{{|\varepsilon |}}{R} = \frac{{17}}{5} = 3.4\,A$
